    Great — the rerun worked and did not crash, which confirms your Step 2a fix solved the WinError 10053 issue.

    You now have:

    • TEI OK: 381 of 389 processed in that run (8 failed).
    • Output TEIs are in: .\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1
    • Log is written at: .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log_round4b.csv

    Now we do the two checks that matter for your review workflow:

    1. What failed (so we can decide re-download vs OCR vs ignore)?
    2. What’s the included→TEI coverage now (so you can start extraction + categorisation)?

    Step 1 — Summarize the run (status counts)

    Run:

    PowerShell
    $log4 = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log_round4b.csv"
Import-Csv $log4 | Group-Object status | Sort-Object Count -Descending | Format-Table Count, Name

    Step 2 — Extract the exact 8 failures + their error messages (actionable list)

    Run:

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv $log4 |
  Where-Object status -eq "failed" |
  Select-Object pdf, error |
  Format-Table -AutoSize

    This will tell us whether each failure is:

    • corrupted/not-a-real-PDF,
    • encrypted,
    • scanned/image-only,
    • timeout/HTTP problem,
    • or something else.

    Then we’ll pick the minimal fix per file.

    Step 3 — Rebuild “included → TEI” map (this is the gate for evidence extraction)

    Run:

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    Paste the printed summary lines (Included rows / PDF found / TEI found via stem).

    Step 4 — Now we apply your inclusion/exclusion + categorisation rules (the reversible way)

    Once Step 3 is pasted, I’ll give you one script:

    .\scripts\apply_scope_categorization_suggestions_v1.py

    It will:

    • read your registry
    • add new columns (does not overwrite include_final):
      • doc_type_suggested
      • scope_bucket_suggested
      • include_suggested
      • reason_excluded_suggested
      • fulltext_tei_available (from your TEI map)
    • write a review sheet CSV you can open in Excel:
      • .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\scope_suggestions_v1.csv

    That’s where we operationalize the exclusion/categorisation rules you asked for.

    Do these 3 commands now and paste outputs

    1. status table
    2. failed pdf+error table
    3. included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py summary

    Then we move into categorisation and exclusion suggestions immediately.

    grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py

    https://gist.github.com/Achionye/a76bef98ec51225b7b20f24cbd9ca145

    Your Step 2a edit is correct: your script now catches the exact failure that stopped you (ConnectionAbortedError / WinError 10053) because the except (...) tuple includes ConnectionAbortedError (and OSError). 1

    You can proceed without doing 2b. The goal now is simply: rerun the batch so the newly added PDFs (from DOCX conversions + additional downloads) get TEIs, then recompute TEI coverage.

    Step 1 — Make sure GROBID is still alive (quick guard)

    From project root:

    PowerShell
    cd C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project

docker ps
Invoke-WebRequest "http://127.0.0.1:8070/api/isalive" -UseBasicParsing -TimeoutSec 10 |
  Select-Object StatusCode, Content

    If isalive fails, do:

    PowerShell
    docker restart grobid

    Step 2 — Re-run your batch TEI build (round4 again, now it shouldn’t crash)

    Use a new log filename so you don’t mix runs:

    PowerShell
    $teiOut = ".\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1"
$log4   = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log_round4b.csv"

python .\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py `
  --pdfdir .\forlitrev_pdfs `
  --outdir $teiOut `
  --log $log4 `
  --retries 5

    What will happen now:

    • Any connection aborts should be logged as failed and the script should continue instead of terminating. 1
    • Re-running is safe because your script skips TEIs that already exist and are >5000 bytes. 1

    Step 3 — Summarize the run + rebuild included→TEI map

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv $log4 | Group-Object status | Sort-Object Count -Descending | Format-Table Count, Name

python .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    Step 4 — If there are still failures: pull the exact failed PDF list (actionable)

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv $log4 | Where-Object status -eq "failed" | Select-Object pdf, error

    At that point, we’ll decide per-file: re-download clean PDF vs decrypt/repair vs OCR (only for the stubborn ones).

    Why I’m confident this is the right “minimal risk” move

    • You already proved the pipeline works end-to-end (you previously got 366/375 ok).
    • The only thing that broke round4 was an uncaught exception; you fixed that.
    • We’re not changing logic, not adding new modules—just rerunning to generate TEIs for the new PDFs you ingested.

    Paste back the two outputs from Step 3 (the status table + the included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py summary).     I effected step 2a. I couldn't get to 2b as the edits are getting too much so I don't risk breaking my code without me and yourself even knowing it. This is the py script now: https://gist.github.com/Achionye/a76bef98ec51225b7b20f24cbd9ca145

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    Gist

    https://gist.githubusercontent.com/Achionye/ca782340e7ab67d7a152c94c00717185/raw/859175f1e3782861e22dc4ee5efd0f19e855e395/grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py

    2

    Oguta review: CORE scripts (text-only)

    https://gist.github.com/Achionye/ca782340e7ab67d7a152c94c00717185

    You’re in good shape: dedupe worked, and your registry update steps worked. The only real blocker now is the GROBID batch crashing because your retry script does not catch ConnectionAbortedError (WinError 10053), so it aborts before it ever writes the round4 log file.

    What just happened (in your exact run)

    • primary_file rewrites from dedupe map: 0 is fine — it simply means no row in your registry was pointing to the duplicate filename you quarantined.
    • Updated primary_file docx->pdf rows: 10 is great — that means 10 registry rows that were pointing to .docx now point to real PDFs in forlitrev_pdfs.
    • Then grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py crashed with ConnectionAbortedError [WinError 10053] → your script catches (HTTPError, URLError, TimeoutError, ConnectionResetError) but not ConnectionAbortedError, so the script terminates and never creates grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log_round4.csv. 1

    Step 1 (now): verify GROBID is alive before re-running

    Run:

    PowerShell
    docker ps
Invoke-WebRequest "http://127.0.0.1:8070/api/isalive" -UseBasicParsing -TimeoutSec 10 |
  Select-Object StatusCode, Content

    If /api/isalive doesn’t return 200 / true, restart the container (since you’re already using Docker successfully):

    PowerShell
    docker restart grobid

    Step 2 (fix the crash properly): patch your retry script to catch ConnectionAbortedError + always write a log

    Edit: .\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py

    2A) Expand the exception tuple

    Find the line that looks like this (from your gist): 1

    Python
    except (HTTPError, URLError, TimeoutError, ConnectionResetError) as e:

    Change it to:

    Python
    except (HTTPError, URLError, TimeoutError, ConnectionResetError, ConnectionAbortedError, OSError) as e:

    That alone prevents the hard crash you saw.

    2B) Make sure a log gets written even if the run dies mid-way (high value)

    Right now, your log is written only later in the script, so a crash means “no CSV exists” (exactly what you experienced). The quick fix is: after you append a row to log_rows for each PDF, write the log immediately.

    Add this helper near the top (after imports):

    Python
    def write_log(path, log_rows):
    keys = []
    seen = set()
    for r in log_rows:
        for k in r.keys():
            if k not in seen:
                seen.add(k); keys.append(k)
    with open(path, "w", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=keys)
        w.writeheader()
        w.writerows(log_rows)

    Then, inside your main PDF loop, right after each log_rows.append(...), call:

    Python
    write_log(args.log, log_rows)

    This makes the run “crash-tolerant”: you’ll always have a partial log to inspect.

    (You don’t have to perfect the script right now—these two edits are enough to proceed.)

    Step 3 (rerun round4 safely)

    Now re-run:

    PowerShell
    $teiOut = ".\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1"
$log4   = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log_round4.csv"

python .\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py `
  --pdfdir .\forlitrev_pdfs `
  --outdir $teiOut `
  --log $log4 `
  --retries 5

    Then:

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv $log4 | Group-Object status | Sort-Object Count -Descending | Format-Table Count, Name
python .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    If you want an immediate workaround (no code edits): use your “missing-only” script

    You already have grobid_retry_missing_tei_v1.py, and it catches broad exceptions and continues (so it won’t die like round4 did). 2

    Run:

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\grobid_retry_missing_tei_v1.py `
  --pdfdir .\forlitrev_pdfs `
  --teidir .\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1

    This is a good “get unstuck now” option, even if you still patch v3 afterward.

    After TEI generation is stable: we do your inclusion/exclusion + categorisation rules

    Once you paste the output of:

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    …I’ll give you the next script that applies suggested categorisation/exclusion rules into new columns (*_suggested) without touching include_final until you approve, exactly as you requested.

    Perfect — your dedupe machinery worked correctly. You have exactly one confirmed byte-identical duplicate pair, you generated a 1-row drop→keep map, and you quarantined exactly 1 duplicate PDF. That’s the clean outcome we want.

    Now we continue from exactly where you are, using PowerShell‑5.1‑compatible steps (no heredocs).

    Step 3 (now) — Rewrite primary_file in your registry using the dedupe map

    This ensures that if any registry row pointed to 1753-Article Text-5330-1-10-20240220.pdf, it will now point to the canonical kept file 10_37284_eajenr_7_1_1753.pdf.

    Run this from project root:

    PowerShell
    cd C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project

$py = @'
import csv, shutil
from pathlib import Path

root = Path(r"C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project")
reg  = root/"screening_v1"/"study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv"
mapf = root/"out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1"/"pdf_dedupe_drop_keep_map_v1.csv"

bak = reg.with_suffix(".csv.bak_before_pdf_dedupe_rewrite")
shutil.copyfile(reg, bak)

drop_to_keep = {}
with open(mapf, encoding="utf-8-sig", newline="") as f:
    for r in csv.DictReader(f):
        drop_to_keep[r["drop_file"]] = r["keep_file"]

rows = list(csv.DictReader(open(reg, encoding="utf-8-sig", newline="")))
changed = 0

for r in rows:
    pf = (r.get("primary_file") or "").strip()
    if pf in drop_to_keep:
        r["primary_file"] = drop_to_keep[pf]
        changed += 1

with open(reg, "w", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=rows[0].keys())
    w.writeheader()
    w.writerows(rows)

print("Backup:", bak)
print("Dedupe map entries:", len(drop_to_keep))
print("primary_file rewrites from dedupe map:", changed)
'@

$scriptPath = ".\_tmp_rewrite_primary_file_from_dedupe_map.py"
$py | Set-Content -Encoding utf8 $scriptPath
python $scriptPath
Remove-Item $scriptPath -Force

    Paste the printed line primary_file rewrites from dedupe map: X after you run it.

    Step 4 (now) — Flip any remaining .docx primary_file entries to their .pdf equivalents

    Since you already copied the converted PDFs into .\forlitrev_pdfs\, this should now succeed.

    PowerShell
    $py = @'
import csv, shutil
from pathlib import Path

root = Path(r"C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project")
reg  = root/"screening_v1"/"study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv"
pdfdir = root/"forlitrev_pdfs"

bak = reg.with_suffix(".csv.bak_docx2pdf_round2")
shutil.copyfile(reg, bak)

rows = list(csv.DictReader(open(reg, encoding="utf-8-sig", newline="")))
changed = 0

for r in rows:
    pf = (r.get("primary_file") or "").strip()
    if pf.lower().endswith(".docx"):
        pdf_name = Path(pf).with_suffix(".pdf").name
        if (pdfdir/pdf_name).exists():
            r["primary_file"] = pdf_name
            changed += 1

with open(reg, "w", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=rows[0].keys())
    w.writeheader()
    w.writerows(rows)

print("Backup:", bak)
print("Updated primary_file docx->pdf rows:", changed)
'@

$scriptPath = ".\_tmp_docx2pdf_swap.py"
$py | Set-Content -Encoding utf8 $scriptPath
python $scriptPath
Remove-Item $scriptPath -Force

    Paste the printed line Updated primary_file docx->pdf rows: Y.

    If Y is 0, that means the converted PDF filenames don’t match the DOCX basenames exactly (very common if Office adds “(1)” etc.). If that happens, we’ll solve it by generating a docx-stem → nearest-pdf mapping automatically.

    Step 5 (now) — Incremental GROBID run to generate TEIs for the newly added PDFs

    Your batch runner will skip TEIs that already exist, so this should mainly process the newly imported PDFs.

    PowerShell
    $teiOut = ".\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1"
$log4   = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log_round4.csv"

python .\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py `
  --pdfdir .\forlitrev_pdfs `
  --outdir $teiOut `
  --log $log4 `
  --retries 3

Import-Csv $log4 | Group-Object status | Sort-Object Count -Descending | Format-Table Count, Name

    Then recompute included→TEI availability:

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    Why we do this before “exclusion/categorisation”

    Because your inclusion/exclusion decisions should be able to say: “full text available (TEI exists) vs not”. That’s a defensible criterion for what you can include in RQ3–RQ6 (validation rigor, matchups, preprocessing transparency, transferability), even if you keep some items for mapping/background.

    Paste back 3 outputs (then I’ll generate your custom categorisation/exclusion rule script)

    1. primary_file rewrites from dedupe map: X
    2. Updated primary_file docx->pdf rows: Y
    3. The two summary outputs:
      • the Group-Object status table for round4
      • the 4-line summary printed by included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    Once we see those, we’ll be ready to apply your scoped rules cleanly:

    • suggest exclusions (e.g., Ramsar RIS, pure DEM hydro-processing) into new columns without touching include_final until you approve,
    • categorise by doc_type_suggested and scope_bucket_suggested for your scoping critical review.

    No Sources

    PowerShell 5.1 doesn’t support the Bash-style heredoc syntax you tried:

    PowerShell
    python - << 'PY'
...
PY

    That’s why you got the redirection/operator errors. In PowerShell, the reliable pattern is: write a temporary .py file with a here-string, then run it.

    Also: your line

    DropKeep map rows:

    printed blank because $map.Count is not a reliable way to count in PS when the variable is $null or not an array. We’ll confirm whether the map is actually empty using Measure-Object.

    Below is the exact “resume from here” sequence, tailored to what you just ran.

    0) Quick verification: did we actually find duplicates + did quarantine move anything?

    Run:

    PowerShell
    # how many duplicate groups?
($dupGroups | Measure-Object).Count

# how many rows in the drop→keep mapping you wrote?
Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\pdf_dedupe_drop_keep_map_v1.csv | Measure-Object

# how many PDFs are in quarantine now?
(Get-ChildItem .\forlitrev_pdfs__dups_quarantine_v1 -File -Filter *.pdf | Measure-Object).Count

# show first few map rows (if any)
Get-Content .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\pdf_dedupe_drop_keep_map_v1.csv -TotalCount 5

    If the map has 0 rows, then you didn’t actually export any duplicates in that run (even though you saw duplicates earlier). In that case, we’ll regenerate the map again cleanly—but do the checks above first so we don’t accidentally proceed on wrong assumptions.

    1) Step 3 (registry rewrite from dedupe map) — PowerShell-compatible

    Create a temp python script file and run it:

    PowerShell
    $py = @'
import csv, shutil
from pathlib import Path

root = Path(r"C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project")
reg  = root/"screening_v1"/"study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv"
mapf = root/"out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1"/"pdf_dedupe_drop_keep_map_v1.csv"

bak = reg.with_suffix(".csv.bak_before_pdf_dedupe_rewrite")
shutil.copyfile(reg, bak)

drop_to_keep = {}
if mapf.exists():
    with open(mapf, encoding="utf-8-sig", newline="") as f:
        for r in csv.DictReader(f):
            drop_to_keep[r["drop_file"]] = r["keep_file"]

rows = list(csv.DictReader(open(reg, encoding="utf-8-sig", newline="")))
changed = 0

for r in rows:
    pf = (r.get("primary_file") or "").strip()
    if pf in drop_to_keep:
        r["primary_file"] = drop_to_keep[pf]
        changed += 1

with open(reg, "w", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=rows[0].keys())
    w.writeheader()
    w.writerows(rows)

print("Backup:", bak)
print("Dedupe map entries:", len(drop_to_keep))
print("primary_file rewrites from dedupe map:", changed)
'@

$scriptPath = ".\_tmp_rewrite_primary_file_from_dedupe_map.py"
$py | Set-Content -Encoding utf8 $scriptPath
python $scriptPath
Remove-Item $scriptPath -Force

    2) Step 4 (docx → pdf primary_file swap) — PowerShell-compatible

    Now that you copied converted PDFs into forlitrev_pdfs, this should flip many primary_file values away from .docx:

    PowerShell
    $py = @'
import csv, shutil
from pathlib import Path

root = Path(r"C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project")
reg  = root/"screening_v1"/"study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv"
pdfdir = root/"forlitrev_pdfs"

bak = reg.with_suffix(".csv.bak_docx2pdf_round2")
shutil.copyfile(reg, bak)

rows = list(csv.DictReader(open(reg, encoding="utf-8-sig", newline="")))
changed = 0

for r in rows:
    pf = (r.get("primary_file") or "").strip()
    if pf.lower().endswith(".docx"):
        pdf_name = Path(pf).with_suffix(".pdf").name
        if (pdfdir/pdf_name).exists():
            r["primary_file"] = pdf_name
            changed += 1

with open(reg, "w", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=rows[0].keys())
    w.writeheader()
    w.writerows(rows)

print("Backup:", bak)
print("Updated primary_file docx->pdf rows:", changed)
'@

$scriptPath = ".\_tmp_docx2pdf_swap.py"
$py | Set-Content -Encoding utf8 $scriptPath
python $scriptPath
Remove-Item $scriptPath -Force

    3) Step 5 — Re-run GROBID incremental (fast)

    PowerShell
    $teiOut = ".\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1"
$log4   = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log_round4.csv"

python .\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py `
  --pdfdir .\forlitrev_pdfs `
  --outdir $teiOut `
  --log $log4 `
  --retries 3

Import-Csv $log4 | Group-Object status | Sort-Object Count -Descending | Format-Table Count, Name

    Then rebuild the included TEI link map:

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    4) After this, we apply inclusion/exclusion + categorisation rules (the way you asked)

    Once TEI coverage stabilizes, I will give you a custom rule engine script that writes:

    • doc_type_suggested
    • scope_bucket_suggested
    • include_suggested
    • reason_excluded_suggested

    …and does not overwrite your current include_final unless you explicitly apply it.

    Paste back only these two outputs (so we proceed immediately)

    1. The three lines printed by the docx→pdf swap run:
    • Updated primary_file docx->pdf rows: ...
    1. The summary from:
    PowerShell
    Import-Csv $log4 | Group-Object status | ...
python .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    That tells me whether we’ve eliminated the DOCX block and how close you are to 268/268 TEI coverage.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> cd C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> $dst = ".\forlitrev_pdfs"
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> $outMap = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\pdf_dedupe_drop_keep_map_v1.csv"
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force (Split-Path $outMap) | Out-Null

    $keep = grp | Sort-Object ` @{Expression={ if (.File -match '^10') {0} else {1} }}, @{Expression={ $_.File.Length }},

    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    Nice — you’ve now ingested the new PDFs (390 total) and you’ve confirmed true byte-identical duplicates via SHA256. That’s exactly the right place to be.

    We proceed in this exact order so you don’t break your primary_file links or TEI coverage:

    1. Quarantine duplicates safely (without deleting) + create a mapping (drop → keep)
    2. Auto-rewrite your registry primary_file using that mapping (so no row points to a quarantined file)
    3. Re-run “docx→pdf swap” again (now that the converted PDFs are inside forlitrev_pdfs)
    4. Run GROBID batch again (incremental) to generate TEIs for the newly added PDFs
    5. Recompute included→TEI coverage
    6. Then we do categorisation + exclusion suggestions (without overwriting your current include_final until you approve)

    Below are the commands/scripts to do steps 1–5 now.

    Step 1 — Build a duplicate “drop→keep” map (deterministic, logged)

    We’ll choose the canonical (keep) filename per hash using a rule that matches your project reality:

    • Prefer filenames that start with 10_ (these align with your paper_id/DOI-style naming and are easiest to reference later)
    • If none starts with 10_, keep the shortest filename (usually the cleanest)

    Run this in PowerShell from project root:

    PowerShell
    cd C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project

$dst = ".\forlitrev_pdfs"
$outMap = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\pdf_dedupe_drop_keep_map_v1.csv"
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force (Split-Path $outMap) | Out-Null

$files = Get-ChildItem $dst -File -Filter *.pdf

$hashRows = $files | ForEach-Object {
  [pscustomobject]@{
    File = $_.Name
    Path = $_.FullName
    Hash = (Get-FileHash $_.FullName -Algorithm SHA256).Hash
    Size = $_.Length
  }
}

$dupGroups = $hashRows | Group-Object Hash | Where-Object Count -gt 1

$map = foreach ($g in $dupGroups) {
  $grp = $g.Group

  $keep = $grp | Sort-Object `
    @{Expression={ if ($_.File -match '^10_') {0} else {1} }}, `
    @{Expression={ $_.File.Length }}, `
    @{Expression={ $_.File }} |
    Select-Object -First 1

  foreach ($d in ($grp | Where-Object File -ne $keep.File)) {
    [pscustomobject]@{
      hash = $g.Name
      keep_file = $keep.File
      drop_file = $d.File
      keep_path = $keep.Path
      drop_path = $d.Path
      size = $keep.Size
    }
  }
}

$map | Export-Csv -NoTypeInformation -Encoding utf8 $outMap
"Drop→Keep map rows: $($map.Count)"
"Wrote: $outMap"

    This produces a reviewable map before anything is moved.

    Step 2 — Quarantine the drop-files (no deletion)

    Now move the duplicates into a quarantine folder (so you can recover them if needed):

    PowerShell
    $quar = ".\forlitrev_pdfs__dups_quarantine_v1"
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force $quar | Out-Null

$map = Import-Csv ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\pdf_dedupe_drop_keep_map_v1.csv"

foreach ($r in $map) {
  $src = Join-Path ".\forlitrev_pdfs" $r.drop_file
  if (Test-Path $src) {
    Move-Item $src $quar -Force
  }
}

"Quarantined duplicates to: $quar"

    Step 3 — Update your registry primary_file so nothing points to quarantined files

    This is critical: if any row had primary_file = 1753-Article Text-....pdf, it must become the kept canonical file.

    Run this Python block:

    PowerShell
    python - << 'PY'
import csv, shutil
from pathlib import Path

root = Path(r"C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project")
reg  = root/"screening_v1"/"study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv"
mapf = root/"out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1"/"pdf_dedupe_drop_keep_map_v1.csv"

bak = reg.with_suffix(".csv.bak_before_pdf_dedupe_rewrite")
shutil.copyfile(reg, bak)

drop_to_keep = {}
with open(mapf, encoding="utf-8-sig", newline="") as f:
    for r in csv.DictReader(f):
        drop_to_keep[r["drop_file"]] = r["keep_file"]

rows = list(csv.DictReader(open(reg, encoding="utf-8-sig", newline="")))
changed = 0

for r in rows:
    pf = (r.get("primary_file") or "").strip()
    if pf in drop_to_keep:
        r["primary_file"] = drop_to_keep[pf]
        changed += 1

with open(reg, "w", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=rows[0].keys())
    w.writeheader()
    w.writerows(rows)

print("Backup:", bak)
print("primary_file rewrites from dedupe map:", changed)
PY

    Step 4 — Re-run DOCX→PDF swap (now that those PDFs exist in forlitrev_pdfs)

    Even if you ran it earlier, run it again now because you just imported new PDFs.

    PowerShell
    python - << 'PY'
import csv, shutil
from pathlib import Path

root = Path(r"C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project")
reg  = root/"screening_v1"/"study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv"
pdfdir = root/"forlitrev_pdfs"

bak = reg.with_suffix(".csv.bak_docx2pdf_round2")
shutil.copyfile(reg, bak)

rows = list(csv.DictReader(open(reg, encoding="utf-8-sig", newline="")))
changed = 0

for r in rows:
    pf = (r.get("primary_file") or "").strip()
    if pf.lower().endswith(".docx"):
        pdf_name = Path(pf).with_suffix(".pdf").name
        if (pdfdir/pdf_name).exists():
            r["primary_file"] = pdf_name
            changed += 1

with open(reg, "w", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=rows[0].keys())
    w.writeheader()
    w.writerows(rows)

print("Backup:", bak)
print("Updated primary_file docx->pdf rows:", changed)
PY

    Step 5 — Run GROBID incremental extraction again (it should be fast)

    Because your v3 script skips existing TEIs, this will mainly process the new PDFs.

    PowerShell
    $teiOut = ".\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1"
$log4   = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log_round4.csv"

python .\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py `
  --pdfdir .\forlitrev_pdfs `
  --outdir $teiOut `
  --log $log4 `
  --retries 3

Import-Csv $log4 | Group-Object status | Sort-Object Count -Descending | Format-Table Count, Name

    Then rebuild your included TEI link map:

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    Next (after you paste one line): inclusion/exclusion + categorisation rules

    Once you paste the 4 summary lines from included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py (especially TEI found via stem), I’ll give you a project-specific rule engine that:

    • writes doc_type_suggested, scope_bucket_suggested, include_suggested, reason_excluded_suggested
    • uses your actual columns (title, doi_resolved, primary_file, plus (if present) bucket, scope_bucket, doc_type)
    • does not overwrite include_final unless you run it with an explicit --apply

    That’s how we make categorisation defensible and reversible.

    Run Steps 1–5 above and paste:

    • the Group-Object status table from round4, and
    • the new output of included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> cd C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> $src1 = "C:\Users\Hp\Downloads\new"
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> $src2 = "C:\Users\Hp\Downloads\additional"
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> $dst = ".\forlitrev_pdfs"
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> # 1) Copy PDFs in (do not overwrite existing)
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> robocopy $src1 $dst *.pdf /XO /XN /XC /NS /NC /NFL /NDL /NJH /NJS

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> robocopy $src2 $dst *.pdf /XO /XN /XC /NS /NC /NFL /NDL /NJH /NJS

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> # 2) Quick count after intake
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> (Get-ChildItem $dst -Filter *.pdf | Measure-Object).Count
    390
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> $hashes = Get-ChildItem .\forlitrev_pdfs -File -Filter *.pdf |

    ForEach-Object {
    $h = (Get-FileHash _.FullName -Algorithm SHA256).Hash [pscustomobject]@{ Hash=$h; File=.Name; Path=$.FullName }
    }
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> $dups = $hashes | Group-Object Hash | Where-Object Count -gt 1
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> $dups | Select-Object -First 5 | ForEach-Object { $_.Group | Format-Table File, Path }

    File Path

    10_37284_eajenr_7_1_1753.pdf C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\forlitrev_pdfs\10_3...
    1753-Article Text-5330-1-10-20240220.pdf C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\forlitrev_pdfs\1753...

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>

    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    Got it. We’ll proceed using your actual project state and your “peculiar realities”:

    • Your project root is still (from earlier runs): C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project
    • You now have new PDFs created from DOCX in: C:\Users\Hp\Downloads\new
    • You have additional publisher PDFs (including MDPI) in: C:\Users\Hp\Downloads\additional
    • You want me to help you apply inclusion/exclusion + categorisation rules, but in a way that is defensible and reversible (so you don’t accidentally destroy decisions already made).

    The right way to do that is:

    1. Intake the new PDFs into forlitrev_pdfs\ with dedupe + logging
    2. Update the registry (primary_file) so each included record points to the new PDF
    3. Run GROBID only on what’s missing (fast)
    4. Apply categorisation + exclusion suggestions into new columns (don’t overwrite your include_final until you approve)

    Below are the exact steps/commands.

    Step 1 — Intake the new PDFs into your project (dedupe + no overwrites)

    Run this from your project root:

    PowerShell
    cd C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project

$src1 = "C:\Users\Hp\Downloads\new"
$src2 = "C:\Users\Hp\Downloads\additional"
$dst  = ".\forlitrev_pdfs"

# 1) Copy PDFs in (do not overwrite existing)
robocopy $src1 $dst *.pdf /XO /XN /XC /NS /NC /NFL /NDL /NJH /NJS
robocopy $src2 $dst *.pdf /XO /XN /XC /NS /NC /NFL /NDL /NJH /NJS

# 2) Quick count after intake
(Get-ChildItem $dst -Filter *.pdf | Measure-Object).Count

    Why this works for your situation:

    • You already have a working pipeline keyed to .\forlitrev_pdfs\
    • We avoid overwriting existing files (which would break primary_file references)

    Optional but highly recommended: dedupe by hash (handles “docx duplicates” turned into same PDF)

    Run this to identify duplicates:

    PowerShell
    $hashes = Get-ChildItem .\forlitrev_pdfs -File -Filter *.pdf |
  ForEach-Object {
    $h = (Get-FileHash $_.FullName -Algorithm SHA256).Hash
    [pscustomobject]@{ Hash=$h; File=$_.Name; Path=$_.FullName }
  }

$dups = $hashes | Group-Object Hash | Where-Object Count -gt 1
$dups | Select-Object -First 5 | ForEach-Object { $_.Group | Format-Table File, Path }

    If duplicates exist, we’ll quarantine them safely (I’ll give you the exact remove/quarantine commands after you confirm the duplicates are truly identical).

    Step 2 — Update your registry so DOCX-backed records point to the new PDFs

    You already have primary_file for all 268 included rows, which is perfect.

    Run this script block to update only rows where:

    • primary_file ends with .docx, and
    • a same-basename .pdf now exists in forlitrev_pdfs
    PowerShell
    python - << 'PY'
import csv, shutil
from pathlib import Path

root = Path(r"C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project")
reg  = root/"screening_v1"/"study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv"
pdfdir = root/"forlitrev_pdfs"

bak = reg.with_suffix(".csv.bak_before_docx_pdf_swap")
shutil.copyfile(reg, bak)

rows = list(csv.DictReader(open(reg, encoding="utf-8-sig", newline="")))
changed = 0

for r in rows:
    pf = (r.get("primary_file") or "").strip()
    if pf.lower().endswith(".docx"):
        pdf_name = Path(pf).with_suffix(".pdf").name
        if (pdfdir/pdf_name).exists():
            r["primary_file"] = pdf_name
            changed += 1

with open(reg, "w", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=rows[0].keys())
    w.writeheader()
    w.writerows(rows)

print("Backup:", bak)
print("Updated docx->pdf primary_file rows:", changed)
PY

    Step 3 — Regenerate TEIs for newly added PDFs (fast incremental run)

    Since your GROBID server is working and your batch script skips existing TEIs, just rerun it:

    PowerShell
    $teiOut = ".\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1"
$log3   = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log_round3.csv"

python .\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py `
  --pdfdir .\forlitrev_pdfs `
  --outdir $teiOut `
  --log $log3 `
  --retries 3

Import-Csv $log3 | Group-Object status | Sort-Object Count -Descending | Format-Table Count, Name

    Then rebuild the included→TEI map (this is your ground truth for “do we have full text?”):

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    You want TEI found via stem as close to 268 as possible.

    Step 4 — Now I help you apply exclusion + categorisation rules (the defensible way)

    You said you haven’t done inclusion/exclusion “as I said” earlier. The correct workflow is:

    • Don’t overwrite include_final automatically.
    • Instead, create new columns:
      • include_suggested
      • reason_excluded_suggested
      • doc_type_suggested
      • scope_bucket_suggested

    Then you can accept/override those suggestions and only then write include_final.

    Categorisation rules (aligned to your new review scope)

    We’ll implement these as automatic suggestions:

    doc_type_suggested

    • journal_article (default for DOIs not matching below)
    • preprint (DOI contains preprints or title/venue indicates preprint)
    • conference (DOI starts with 10.1109/ or title contains “Proceedings”, “Conference”, etc.)
    • book_chapter (filename/title includes bookchapter or metadata indicates chapter)
    • report/grey (Ramsar RIS, agency reports, untraceable “Overview…” without DOI/venue)

    scope_bucket_suggested (for your RS–ML inland-lake WQ scope)

    • core_rs_ml_inland_wq (remote sensing + ML + water-quality parameter retrieval in inland lake/reservoir)
    • rs_inland_wq_no_ml (RS water quality but no ML; keep for background/comparison)
    • ml_wq_no_rs (ML water quality but not using RS; usually exclude unless it’s methodological framework you want)
    • gis_context_only (DEM/hydro-processing not tied to RS–ML WQ retrieval; usually exclude)
    • review_framework (reviews; keep but label)
    • context_oguta_grey (Ramsar RIS etc.; keep as context anchor, not counted in RS–ML evidence)

    include_suggested

    • 1 for: core_rs_ml_inland_wq, review_framework, (optionally) rs_inland_wq_no_ml
    • 0 for: gis_context_only (e.g., “Digital Elevation Model Based Hydro-processing” looks like this), context_oguta_grey (for quantitative RQ3–RQ6), ml_wq_no_rs (unless you choose otherwise)

    This gives you the “mapping → diagnosis” separation you want.

    Next action to enable this (no guessing about your current filled fields)

    Run this and paste 5 sample rows (so I can see what’s already populated in your columns like doc_type, bucket, scope_bucket, etc.):

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv .\screening_v1\study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv |
  Select-Object -First 5 paper_id, include_final, doc_type, scope_bucket, title, year_verified, doi_resolved, primary_file

    Then I’ll give you an exact apply_scope_categorization_rules_v1.py script that:

    • reads your registry,
    • writes suggestions into new columns,
    • writes a reviewable CSV: out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\scope_suggestions_v1.csv,
    • and does not touch your include_final unless you run it with --apply.

    Immediate next step (so we don’t stall)

    Run Steps 1–3 now (intake → docx→pdf primary_file swap → grobid round3 → included TEI map). Then paste the printed summary line from:

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    Once TEI coverage is stable, we lock categorisation rules and proceed to chunking/extraction.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    I have not done the exclusion/inclusion as you said. You will help me apply the exclusion and categorisation rules. I think you can do that.

    I converted the docx files to pdf using powershell command line for office. The pdf files are all in the folder "C:\Users\Hp\Downloads\new" with the original docx documents. The docx documents seem to have duplication. 4 of the mdpi articles have been downloaded. They're in the folder yes. the files are in "C:\Users\Hp\Downloads\additional"

    Let us proceed.

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    Mdpi

    https://www.mdpi.com/journal/remotesensing//

    2

    Mdpi

    https://www.mdpi.com/2073-4441/17/2/253

    3

    Ramsar

    https://www.ramsar.org/document/strategic-framework-guidelines-future-development-list-wetlands-international-importance-1

    4

    Home | Ramsar Sites Information Service

    https://rsis.ramsar.org/

    You can “get” (i.e., make usable in your pipeline) these 14 items without hunting the entire internet by applying a three-lane treatment that matches what you actually have:

    • Lane 1 (DOCX → replace with official PDF OR convert): 8 items
    • Lane 2 (PDF but GROBID failed → re-download clean PDF / de-encrypt / OCR): 6 items
    • Lane 3 (Non-article PDFs like Ramsar RIS): keep, but treat as context/grey, not part of the RS–ML methods evidence base unless you explicitly want it.

    Below is an exact, project-specific playbook.

    Lane 1 — The 8 DOCX items: fix = “turn into real PDFs”

    These are currently not “scarce”; they are simply not in a format GROBID ingests.

    A. Replace with publisher PDFs (best)

    From your DOCX list:

    • 10_3390_s16081298 (MDPI Sensors) → MDPI pages provide an official Download PDF for their OA papers. 1
    • 10_3390_su15021410 (MDPI Sustainability) → MDPI provides official PDFs for OA papers. 2
    • 10_3390_rs14246238 (MDPI Remote Sensing) → MDPI provides official PDFs; OA. 1
    • 10_20944_preprints202309_0489_v1 (Preprints.org) → the preprint landing page provides downloadable files (PDF); note it’s not peer‑reviewed (you should tag it as preprint in your registry). 3

    Action (manual but fast): open DOI in browser → download PDF → save into .\forlitrev_pdfs\ → update primary_file to the PDF filename.

    B. If you can’t access the official PDF quickly: convert DOCX → PDF locally (fast unblocker)

    Install LibreOffice once, then:

    PowerShell
    $soffice = "C:\Program Files\LibreOffice\program\soffice.exe"
$outdir  = (Resolve-Path .\forlitrev_pdfs).Path

Get-ChildItem .\forlitrev_pdfs -File -Filter *.docx | ForEach-Object {
  & $soffice --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir $outdir $_.FullName
}

    Then update your registry primary_file from .docx → .pdf (you already have the docx→pdf updater script snippet from earlier), and rerun GROBID batch (it will skip existing TEIs).

    Lane 2 — The 6 PDFs: “why TEI missing?” = they’re likely not parseable PDFs (or are encrypted/scanned)

    Your 9 GROBID failures list includes items like:

    • remotesensing-12-03157.pdf (MDPI) — if GROBID failed this, the file is often corrupted or not a true PDF download (e.g., HTML saved as .pdf). MDPI provides clean OA PDFs; re-download from the official page. 1
    • Machine Learning Application in Water Quality Using Satellite Data - IOPscience.pdf — these sometimes download as viewer wrappers depending on how saved; re-download using the “Download PDF” button while authenticated.

    Step 2.1: Verify “real PDF” magic bytes for each failure (10 seconds per file)

    PowerShell
    $bad = @(
 "10_34133_remotesensing_0049.pdf",
 "AdvancesinWaterResourcePlanningandSustainabilityBook-bookchapter2023-SSRs.pdf",
 "advancing-water-quality-monitoring-through-artificial-neural-26m35oe3.pdf",
 "Digital Elevation Model Based Hydro‐processing_ Geocarto International_ Vol 21, No 1.pdf",
 "Machine Learning Application in Water Quality Using Satellite Data - IOPscience.pdf",
 "remotesensing-12-03157.pdf",
 "rsis-ramsar-org-ri....pdf",
 "Uncertainty of atmospheric correction algorithms for chlorophyll α concentration retrieval in lakes from Sentinel-2 ...pdf",
 "Water Quality Assessment Using Normalized Difference Index by Applying Remote Sensing Techniques_ Case of Lake Nokou...pdf"
)

foreach ($f in $bad) {
  $p = Join-Path ".\forlitrev_pdfs" $f
  if (Test-Path $p) {
    $b = Get-Content $p -Encoding Byte -TotalCount 4
    $sig = [System.Text.Encoding]::ASCII.GetString($b)
    "{0} => {1}" -f $f, $sig
  } else {
    "{0} => MISSING" -f $f
  }
}
    • If signature ≠ %PDF → re-download (it’s not a real PDF).
    • If it is %PDF but GROBID fails → try decrypt/repair or OCR.

    Step 2.2: If encrypted, decrypt with qpdf (common fix)

    Install qpdf (Windows), then:

    PowerShell
    qpdf --show-encryption ".\forlitrev_pdfs\FILE.pdf"
qpdf --decrypt ".\forlitrev_pdfs\FILE.pdf" ".\forlitrev_pdfs\FILE.decrypted.pdf"

    Then run GROBID on the decrypted copy (or rename it to replace the original and update primary_file).

    Step 2.3: If scanned/image-only, OCR only those few PDFs

    This is the correct moment to introduce OCRmyPDF—but do it only for the handful of persistent failures.

    (If OCRmyPDF won’t install under Python 3.14, use a Python 3.12 venv just for OCR.)

    Lane 3 — Ramsar / RSIS PDFs are not journal “papers”

    You have rsis-ramsar-org-ri....pdf. That is likely a Ramsar Information Sheet (RIS) download from the Ramsar Sites Information Service, which is a legitimate contextual document but not part of the RS–ML methods literature. 4

    Recommendation: keep it in your project as “context/grey literature,” but do not count it in RQ3–RQ6 extraction (validation rigor, matchup N, AC methods, etc.). Use it in the Oguta context section (e.g., threats/pressures, designation facts) instead.

    The fastest path to finish (in your project)

    1. Convert/replace the 8 DOCX → PDFs (this will likely remove most of the 14 no-TEI cases).
    2. Re-download clean PDFs for the MDPI/IOP/IEEE items that failed (start with remotesensing-12-03157.pdf).
    3. Run GROBID batch again (it will be quick because it skips existing TEIs).
    4. Re-run:
      • included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    One thing I need (so I can tell you exactly which of the “6 PDFs” overlap the 9 failures)

    Your log output you pasted earlier only showed the pdf column, not the error column. Run this and paste it:

    PowerShell
    $log = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log.csv"
Import-Csv $log | Where-Object status -eq "failed" | Select-Object pdf, error

    With the error strings, I’ll tell you per file: re-download vs decrypt vs OCR vs exclude (non-article RIS)—no guesswork.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    pdf

    10_34133_remotesensing_0049.pdf
    AdvancesinWaterResourcePlanningandSustainabilityBook-bookchapter2023-SSRs.pdf
    advancing-water-quality-monitoring-through-artificial-neural-26m35oe3.pdf
    Digital Elevation Model Based Hydro‐processing_ Geocarto International_ Vol 21, No 1.pdf
    Machine Learning Application in Water Quality Using Satellite Data - IOPscience.pdf
    remotesensing-12-03157.pdf
    rsis-ramsar-org-ri....pdf
    Uncertainty of atmospheric correction algorithms for chlorophyll α concentration retrieval in lakes from Sentinel-2 ...
    Water Quality Assessment Using Normalized Difference Index by Applying Remote Sensing Techniques_ Case of Lake Nokou...

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    Mdpi

    https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/16/8/1298

    2

    Mdpi

    https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/15/2/1410

    3

    Mdpi

    https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/14/24/6238

    4

    Water quality grade identification for lakes in middle reaches of Yangtze River using landsat-8 data with deep neural networks (DNN) model - CentAUR

    https://centaur.reading.ac.uk/109769/

    5

    Preprints

    https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202309.0489/v1

    6

    Water quality monitoring of Lake Burullus (Egypt) using Landsat satellite imageries - PubMed

    https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33237559/

    7

    Mdpi

    https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/12/19/3157

    You don’t actually have a “scarcity of papers” problem here—you have a format + provenance problem in a list of 14 included items:

    • 8 are .docx → GROBID will never generate TEI from them unless you replace them with a real publisher PDF or convert to PDF.
    • 6 are .pdf but TEI wasn’t produced → these are almost certainly a subset of your earlier 9 GROBID failures (corrupt/encrypted/scan/HTML-wrapper PDFs), and you fix them by re-downloading the “real PDF” and/or OCR.

    Below is the most direct, project-specific way to “get” them (in practice: replace/normalize them into real PDFs your pipeline can ingest).

    A) Fix the 8 DOCX items (the quickest win)

    1) Replace DOCX with the official OA PDF (best for rigor)

    From your DOCX list, at least 4 are definitely available as official OA PDFs:

    1. 10_3390_s16081298 (“A Comprehensive Review on Water Quality Parameters…”)
      MDPI article page has Download PDF. 1

    2. 10_3390_su15021410 (sustainability-15-01410.docx)
      MDPI page has Download PDF. 2

    3. 10_3390_rs14246238 (“Wei et al. 2022…”)
      MDPI page has Download PDF. 3
      Also mirrored as an open-access deposit on University of Reading’s repository (backup source if needed). 4

    4. 10_20944_preprints202309_0489_v1 (Preprints.org manuscript)
      The Preprints page explicitly shows “Download Files” and flags it as not peer-reviewed. 5

    What to do (workflow):

    • Download each of those PDFs to .\forlitrev_pdfs\
    • Update study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv → primary_file for those rows to the new PDF filename
    • Re-run your GROBID batch (it will skip existing TEIs and only process the new PDFs)

    2) For the remaining DOCX items (likely paywalled / unclear): choose one of two defensible paths

    1. 10_1007_s11356_020_11765_1 (Lake Burullus paper)
      This is a Springer journal article (PubMed listing confirms DOI and bibliographic record). 6
      How to “get it”:
    • Try SpringerLink via your institution/library (or ILL)
    • If you can’t access the Version of Record: email corresponding author for an accepted manuscript
    • If full text remains unavailable, keep it for mapping-only (RQ1/RQ2) and mark fulltext_available=0 for critical appraisal extraction (RQ3–RQ6)

    1. 10_1016_j_ecolind_2015_12_009 and 10_1016_j_proenv_2011_09_268 (Elsevier)
      Often accessible via institutional subscription; some conference/proceedings content may be OA depending on venue.
      Same playbook: DOI → library resolver → ILL/author copy if needed.

    2. Overview_of_the_Application_of_Remote_Sensing_in_Effective (no DOI in your registry)
      This is grey/untraceable as currently represented. You have three clean options:

    • Exclude it from included set (best academically, if you can’t identify a stable source)
    • Treat it explicitly as grey literature (keep for background narrative only, not for methodological counts)
    • If you insist on keeping it in extraction: convert DOCX → PDF and run GROBID, but label it clearly as non-journal/uncertain provenance

    B) Fix the 6 PDFs that still have no TEI (these are “bad PDFs”, not “scarce papers”)

    Your PDF list includes at least one that is definitely obtainable as a clean OA PDF:

    • 10_3390_rs12193157 (remotesensing-12-03157.pdf) is an MDPI OA article with Download PDF on the article page. 7
      If GROBID couldn’t TEI it, your local file is likely corrupted or not a true PDF (common: HTML saved as .pdf, partial download, etc.). Re-download the official PDF and rerun GROBID.

    For the other PDF items (Taylor & Francis / IEEE / IOP / InterConf-style proceedings), the pipeline reason TEI is missing is typically one of:

    • encrypted PDF
    • malformed/corrupt PDF
    • scanned-image PDF with no text layer
    • nonstandard PDF object structure that GROBID chokes on

    Do this now: pull the exact GROBID error messages for your 6 PDFs

    This tells you whether you need re-download vs OCR vs “can’t parse”.

    PowerShell
    $log = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log.csv"

# show the failed rows from the batch run
Import-Csv $log |
  Where-Object { $_.status -eq "failed" } |
  Select-Object pdf, error

    Then we’ll match those failures against your 6 PDFs and choose the right fix per file.

    Quick “is this a real PDF?” check (catches HTML-wrapper downloads instantly)

    PowerShell
    # prints the first 4 bytes; should be "%PDF"
Get-Content ".\forlitrev_pdfs\remotesensing-12-03157.pdf" -Encoding Byte -TotalCount 4

    If it’s not %PDF, re-download from the publisher.

    C) The fastest way to make this “not limited” going forward: automatic OA resolver (Unpaywall)

    For DOIs like your MDPI + Preprints items, you can largely eliminate manual hunting by adding one small step:

    • For each DOI in a “missing/fulltext-needed” list, query Unpaywall to get url_for_pdf when an OA copy exists.
    • Download that PDF into forlitrev_pdfs, update primary_file, rerun GROBID.

    This doesn’t solve paywalled items, but it removes a huge amount of friction for OA-heavy RS/ML literature.

    What to do next (so we proceed cleanly, step-by-step)

    Paste the output of the failed log query (the pdf, error table). With that, I’ll tell you exactly for each of the 6 PDFs whether the fix is:

    • re-download official PDF (MDPI / publisher)
    • OCR (scanned)
    • decrypt / re-save (encrypted/malformed)
    • or exclude / map-only (if it’s inaccessible)

    And once DOCX→PDF replacements are done, you’ll rerun:

    • grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py (round 2)
    • included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    and you should end up very close to 268/268 included with TEI (or with every remaining gap explicitly justified in your Methods/Limitations).

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    paper_id doi_resolved primary_file

    10_3390_s16081298 A Comprehensive Review on Water Quality.docx
    10_20944_preprints202309_0489_v1 A_Review_of_Remote_Sensing_Applications_for_...
    Overview_of_the_Application_of_Remote_Sensing_in_Effective Overview of the Application of Remote Sensin...
    10_1016_j_ecolind_2015_12_009 Remote Sensing for lake.docx
    10_1016_j_proenv_2011_09_268 Research on the Water Quality Monitoring Sys...
    10_3390_su15021410 sustainability-15-01410.docx
    10_1007_s11356_020_11765_1 Water quality monitoring of Lake Burullus (E...

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> "---- PDF ----"
    ---- PDF ----
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> $wl | Where-Object { $_.primary_file.ToLower().EndsWith(".pdf") } |

    Format-Table paper_id, doi_resolved, primary_file, title -AutoSize

    paper_id doi_resolved primary_file

    10_51582_interconf_19_20_04_2023_041 advancing-water-quality-monitoring-through-artificial-neural-26m35...
    10_1080_10106040608542370 Digital Elevation Model Based Hydro‐processing_ Geocarto Internati...
    10_1088_1755_1315_842_1_012018 Machine Learning Application in Water Quality Using Satellite Data...
    10_3390_rs12193157 remotesensing-12-03157.pdf

    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    Good—this is no longer “scarcity,” it’s a pipeline format + last‑mile acquisition problem:

    • 8 items are .docx → GROBID will never produce TEI from those until you replace them with PDFs or convert them to PDF.
    • 6 items are .pdf but still no TEI → these are either (a) the earlier GROBID failures, (b) PDFs added after the batch, or (c) PDFs that need OCR / are malformed.

    Below is the exact step-by-step path that will get you to ~268/268 TEI coverage (or at least make every remaining gap explicitly explainable).

    Step 1 — Print the exact 8 DOCX and 6 PDF rows (so you act on a concrete list)

    PowerShell
    $wl = Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_no_tei_worklist_v1.csv

"---- DOCX ----"
$wl | Where-Object { $_.primary_file.ToLower().EndsWith(".docx") } |
  Format-Table paper_id, doi_resolved, primary_file, title -AutoSize

"---- PDF ----"
$wl | Where-Object { $_.primary_file.ToLower().EndsWith(".pdf") } |
  Format-Table paper_id, doi_resolved, primary_file, title -AutoSize

    This is your “to-fix” list.

    Step 2 — Resolve the 8 DOCX items (choose one of two correct approaches)

    Option A (best, most rigorous): Replace DOCX with the publisher PDF

    This is ideal for your review because:

    • you preserve pagination, figures, DOI header text
    • GROBID extraction is cleaner
    • it’s more reproducible/auditable as “the paper”

    How to do it systematically (not manual hunting):

    • For each DOCX row that has a DOI (doi_resolved not empty), try to download the official PDF from the DOI landing page using your browser / institutional access / OA sources.
    • Save into .\forlitrev_pdfs\ and set primary_file to that PDF filename.

    (If you want, the next step after this is an automated OA resolver/downloader using Unpaywall/OpenAlex—but you don’t need it to proceed right now.)

    Option B (fastest unblocker): Convert DOCX → PDF locally, then GROBID the PDFs

    This is usually enough to unblock TEI generation.

    B1) Convert using LibreOffice (recommended for headless conversion)

    Install LibreOffice once, then run:

    PowerShell
    $soffice = "C:\Program Files\LibreOffice\program\soffice.exe"
$outdir  = (Resolve-Path .\forlitrev_pdfs).Path

Get-ChildItem .\forlitrev_pdfs -File -Filter *.docx | ForEach-Object {
  & $soffice --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir $outdir $_.FullName
}

    B2) Update your registry primary_file from .docx to the new .pdf

    Run this (safe: creates a backup first):

    PowerShell
    python - << 'PY'
import csv, shutil
from pathlib import Path

root = Path(r"C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project")
reg = root/"screening_v1"/"study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv"
bak = reg.with_suffix(".csv.bak_docx2pdf")
shutil.copyfile(reg, bak)

pdfdir = root/"forlitrev_pdfs"
rows = list(csv.DictReader(open(reg, encoding="utf-8-sig", newline="")))
changed = 0

for r in rows:
    pf = (r.get("primary_file") or "").strip()
    if pf.lower().endswith(".docx"):
        pdf_name = Path(pf).with_suffix(".pdf").name
        if (pdfdir/pdf_name).exists():
            r["primary_file"] = pdf_name
            changed += 1

with open(reg, "w", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=rows[0].keys())
    w.writeheader()
    w.writerows(rows)

print("Backed up:", bak)
print("Updated primary_file docx->pdf rows:", changed)
PY

    Step 3 — Re-run GROBID batch (it will fill in only what’s missing)

    Because your grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py skips existing TEIs, you can safely rerun it on the whole folder and it won’t redo 366 files.

    PowerShell
    $teiOut = ".\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1"
$log2   = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log_round2.csv"

python .\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py `
  --pdfdir .\forlitrev_pdfs `
  --outdir $teiOut `
  --log $log2 `
  --retries 3

Import-Csv $log2 | Group-Object status | Sort-Object Count -Descending | Format-Table Count, Name

    Step 4 — Fix the 6 “PDF but no TEI” cases (true extraction failures)

    After Step 3, re-run:

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    If any PDFs still show tei_exists=0, they are true trouble PDFs. The next actions are:

    4A) Identify them precisely (paths + titles)

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.csv |
  Where-Object { $_.pdf_exists -eq "1" -and $_.tei_exists -eq "0" } |
  Format-Table paper_id, doi_resolved, primary_file, pdf_path_resolved, title -AutoSize

    4B) For persistent failures: OCR only those PDFs, then retry GROBID

    This is the “not limited” solution, but OCRmyPDF may not support Python 3.14 yet. If it gives install issues, do it in a Python 3.12 venv.

    High-level flow:

    1. OCR these PDFs into forlitrev_pdfs_ocr\
    2. point primary_file to the OCR’d copies (or temporarily run GROBID on that folder)
    3. rerun GROBID only on that small set

    (Once you paste the list from 4A, I’ll give you the exact OCR commands tailored to those filenames, and a minimal retry script that only submits those PDFs to the GROBID API.)

    Step 5 — The 1 missing local file (pdf_exists=0)

    You already exported:

    • .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_missing_local_file_v1.csv

    Open it and you’ll usually find the issue is simply: primary_file points to a filename that doesn’t exist (rename mismatch) OR you never downloaded it.

    Quick check:

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_missing_local_file_v1.csv |
  Format-Table paper_id, doi_resolved, primary_file, title -AutoSize

    Then:

    • if it’s a rename issue → rename file or fix primary_file
    • if it’s truly not downloaded → download PDF via DOI / library / author copy

    What to do next (so we proceed without guessing)

    Run Step 1 (the printing of DOCX and PDF rows) and paste that output. With the actual 8+6 filenames in front of me, I’ll give you the shortest exact action plan, e.g.:

    • “Convert these 8 DOCX to PDF; update registry; rerun GROBID; now only these 2 PDFs need OCR; this 1 paper needs download.”

    That will get you from 253 TEIs to essentially full included coverage.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    Where-Object { $.pdf_exists -eq "0" } |
    Export-Csv -NoTypeInformation -Encoding utf8 .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_missing_local_file_v1.csv
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> # Included but no TEI (tei_exists=0)
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> Copy-Item .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_pdf_no_tei_v1.csv `
    .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_no_tei_worklist_v1.csv -Force
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_no_tei_worklist_v1.csv |
    ForEach-Object {
    ∣Add−Member−NotePropertyNameext−NotePropertyValue([IO.Path]::GetExtension(_ | Add-Member -NotePropertyName ext -NotePropertyValue ([IO.Path]::GetExtension(∣​Add−Member−NotePropertyNameext−NotePropertyValue([IO.Path]::GetExtension(    .primary_file).ToLower()) -Force
    $_
    } |
    Group-Object ext | Sort-Object Count -Descending |
    Format-Table Count, Name

    Count Name

    text
    8 .docx
6 .pdf

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>

    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    You’re down to a manageable acquisition/format problem:

    • 1 included record has no local file (pdf_exists=0).
    • 14 included records have a local file but no TEI (tei_exists=0). In your sample, several of these are .docx, which explains “no TEI” immediately (GROBID batch only processes PDFs).

    So “how do I get these papers?” splits into (A) get/replace the right full-text PDF, and (B) make your pipeline accept what you already have (DOCX→PDF), plus (C) handle a few true PDF failures.

    Step 1 — Generate a single “worklist” (the only list you will act on)

    Run this to export both the 1 missing file and the 14 no-TEI cases into one CSV:

    PowerShell
    # Missing local file (pdf_exists=0)
Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.csv |
  Where-Object { $_.pdf_exists -eq "0" } |
  Export-Csv -NoTypeInformation -Encoding utf8 .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_missing_local_file_v1.csv

# Included but no TEI (tei_exists=0)
Copy-Item .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_pdf_no_tei_v1.csv `
  .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_no_tei_worklist_v1.csv -Force

    Now you have exactly 15 items to fix, not “268 papers”.

    Step 2 — Classify the 14 “no TEI” items: DOCX vs PDF (this tells you what “getting papers” actually means)

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_no_tei_worklist_v1.csv |
  ForEach-Object {
    $_ | Add-Member -NotePropertyName ext -NotePropertyValue ([IO.Path]::GetExtension($_.primary_file).ToLower()) -Force
    $_
  } |
  Group-Object ext | Sort-Object Count -Descending |
  Format-Table Count, Name

    Interpretation:

    • If many are .docx: you don’t need “scarce resources”; you need publisher PDFs (best) or DOCX→PDF conversion (acceptable fallback).
    • If some are .pdf: those are either the 9 GROBID failures or edge PDFs that need OCR/retry.

    Step 3 — Get PDFs in a way that won’t limit you (legal + reproducible)

    3A) Fast path for Open Access PDFs (automation-friendly)

    Add one tool to your toolkit: Unpaywall (free API; no key). It often returns a direct OA PDF URL for a DOI.

    What you do: for each DOI in the worklist, call Unpaywall → if it returns best_oa_location.url_for_pdf, download it into forlitrev_pdfs\, update primary_file.

    This is the single most effective “not limited” improvement because it turns “hunt papers manually” into “resolve OA link → download”.

    (You already use OpenAlex; OpenAlex also provides OA locations sometimes, but Unpaywall is purpose-built for OA PDF resolution.)

    3B) If not OA: institutional access / ILL / author copy (still reproducible)

    For paywalled DOIs, the clean options are:

    • University/library “Find it @ …” via DOI
    • Interlibrary loan
    • Author accepted manuscript via email / institutional repository

    Pipeline-wise, you can still keep the record in your mapping results, but mark fulltext_available=0 if you can’t obtain the PDF.

    Step 4 — Eliminate DOCX as a blocker (convert to PDF or replace with publisher PDF)

    Best: replace DOCX with official publisher PDF

    If the DOI exists, prefer the official PDF.

    Fallback: convert DOCX → PDF locally (so GROBID can run)

    Install LibreOffice once, then:

    PowerShell
    $soffice = "C:\Program Files\LibreOffice\program\soffice.exe"
$outdir  = (Resolve-Path .\forlitrev_pdfs).Path

Get-ChildItem .\forlitrev_pdfs -Filter *.docx | ForEach-Object {
  & $soffice --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir $outdir $_.FullName
}

    Then point your registry primary_file to the new .pdf (same basename), and rerun GROBID only for those PDFs.

    This alone will likely wipe out most of the 14.

    Step 5 — Retry TEI generation only for the remaining failures (don’t re-run 375)

    Once the DOCX issue is solved, re-run TEI generation only for the problem PDFs:

    1. Create a “retry PDF list” (rows where tei_exists=0 and ext is .pdf):
    PowerShell
    Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.csv |
  Where-Object { $_.pdf_exists -eq "1" -and $_.tei_exists -eq "0" } |
  Select-Object -ExpandProperty pdf_path_resolved
    1. If they’re scanned/image PDFs, add OCRmyPDF (this is the other “not limited” tool) and OCR only those, then rerun GROBID.

    What I need next (one paste) to give you the exact, custom “download/convert/retry” plan

    Paste the output of this, which shows exactly which of the 14 are DOCX vs PDF:

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_no_tei_worklist_v1.csv |
  Select-Object paper_id, doi_resolved, primary_file, pdf_path_resolved

    With that, I’ll tell you:

    • which ones are simply “convert docx → pdf”,
    • which ones need “download publisher PDF by DOI”,
    • which ones match your 9 GROBID failures and should go through OCR/retry,
    • and I’ll give you a small Python script (tailored to your registry columns) that uses Unpaywall to fetch OA PDFs automatically where possible.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    Count : 14

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    Assessment of heavy metal and physico-chemical pollution loadings of River Benue water at Makurdi using water quality index (WQI) and multivariate statistics - Applied Water Science

    https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s13201-021-01456-8

    2

    Mdpi

    https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/16/8/1298

    3

    Mdpi

    https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/12/19/3157/notes

    4

    Preprints

    https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202309.0489/v1

    5

    Procedia Environmental Sciences | 2011 International Conference on Environment Science and Biotechnology (ICESB 2011) | ScienceDirect.com by Elsevier

    https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/procedia-environmental-sciences/vol/8

    Most of what you’re calling “scarce papers” in your included_pdf_no_tei_v1.csv are not actually “missing from the world”—they’re missing from your pipeline for two very specific reasons:

    1. some of your “PDFs” are actually .docx, which GROBID cannot process, and
    2. you have 1 included record whose PDF is genuinely missing locally (pdf_exists=0), plus a small number of PDFs where TEI wasn’t produced.

    So the right move is a format + acquisition cleanup, not a new search strategy.

    1) Fix the 1 truly missing local PDF (pdf_exists=0)

    From your output:

    • paper_id = 10_1007_s13201_021_01456_8 (Springer Applied Water Science)
    • You currently don’t have the file locally (pdf_exists=0).

    Good news: this specific paper is Open Access on SpringerLink and the page explicitly says “You have full access” and provides Download PDF. 1

    Action: download the PDF from SpringerLink, save it into .\forlitrev_pdfs\, and update the registry primary_file to that filename. Then re-run your GROBID batch just for that PDF (or rerun the batch with “skip existing” behavior).

    2) Replace the .docx “papers” with the publisher PDFs (this will wipe out a big chunk of “no TEI”)

    Your included_pdf_no_tei_v1.csv shows multiple entries like:

    • A Comprehensive Review on Water Quality.docx with DOI 10.3390/s16081298
    • A_Review_of_Remote_Sensing_Applications_for_Determ.docx with DOI 10.20944/preprints202309.0489.v1
    • Research on the Water Quality Monitoring System for Inland.docx with DOI 10.1016/j.proenv.2011.09.268

    These already exist as official PDFs online:

    (a) MDPI (Open Access) → direct PDF available

    Your DOI 10.3390/s16081298 is an MDPI Sensors article page (open access), and MDPI provides the official article page (from which you download the PDF). 2

    Your DOI 10.3390/rs12193157 (MDPI Remote Sensing) also has an official PDF endpoint shown in MDPI’s version notes (link ends with /pdf). 3

    Action: download the official PDFs from MDPI and store them as .pdf in forlitrev_pdfs (then point primary_file to them). This is better than keeping .docx because it preserves page structure, figures, and the DOI in header—GROBID extracts cleaner TEI.

    (b) Preprints.org (Open Access) → “Download Files” on the preprint page

    Your DOI 10.20944/preprints202309.0489.v1 resolves to a Preprints.org page that clearly shows Download Files for the preprint. 4

    Action: download the PDF from that page, save it as .pdf, and update primary_file. (Also note: it’s a preprint—your review should tag it as such.)

    (c) Procedia Environmental Sciences (ScienceDirect) → Open Access volume with “View PDF”

    Procedia Environmental Sciences conference volumes on ScienceDirect are listed as Open access and provide “View PDF” links at the volume/article level. 5

    Action: resolve 10.1016/j.proenv.2011.09.268 via the DOI, download the PDF from ScienceDirect, store locally, update primary_file.

    3) What to do for genuinely hard-to-get (paywalled / obscure) tropical resources

    Some tropical-context papers will be legitimately difficult (regional journals, older Taylor & Francis, some IEEE/IOP items depending on access). For those, the academically clean options are:

    1. Institutional library access by DOI (often works even when Google doesn’t).
    2. Interlibrary loan (ILL) — surprisingly effective for older/obscure items.
    3. Author request (email corresponding author; ask for “author accepted manuscript” if the VoR is paywalled).
    4. Institutional repositories (many authors deposit accepted manuscripts).
    5. Adjust your review’s extraction tier:
      • keep the record in the mapping dataset (RQ1/RQ2) if you have metadata/abstract,
      • but mark fulltext_available=0 so it’s excluded from “validation rigor / preprocessing transparency” extraction (RQ3–RQ6).
        That is a normal, defensible scoping-review practice—just report it in limitations.

    (Do not use piracy sources; it creates ethical and reproducibility problems for the manuscript.)

    4) Make this systematic in your project (so you don’t chase papers manually)

    You already have the right tables to automate this.

    4.1 Split your “no TEI” list into: DOCX vs PDF problems

    Run:

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_pdf_no_tei_v1.csv |
  Group-Object { ([IO.Path]::GetExtension($_.primary_file)).ToLower() } |
  Format-Table Count, Name
    • Everything ending in .docx should be handled as “replace with official PDF OR convert to PDF”.
    • Everything ending in .pdf should be handled as “GROBID failure / retry / OCR”.

    4.2 If you can’t get an official PDF, convert DOCX → PDF locally (pipeline-compatible)

    Install LibreOffice (once), then:

    PowerShell
    $soffice = "C:\Program Files\LibreOffice\program\soffice.exe"
$outdir  = "C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\forlitrev_pdfs"

Get-ChildItem .\forlitrev_pdfs -Filter *.docx | ForEach-Object {
  & $soffice --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir $outdir $_.FullName
}

    Then run your GROBID batch again (it will pick up the new PDFs).

    This is not as clean as getting the publisher PDF, but it will unblock TEI extraction.

    5) The key idea: “scarce topic” ≠ “scarce access”

    Your list already includes several open-access items (MDPI, Springer OA, Preprints.org, Procedia OA). 2
    So you’re not blocked by global availability; you’re blocked by (a) format drift (docx in a PDF pipeline) and (b) local missing file + a few failures.

    If you paste the output of this (just counts), I’ll give you the exact next command sequence to reach ~268/268 TEI coverage:

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_pdf_no_tei_v1.csv | Measure-Object

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> python .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py
    Included rows: 268
    Have primary_file: 268
    PDF found: 267
    TEI found via stem: 253

    10_1117_1_jrs_14_034520,,Global chlorophyll-a concentration estimation from moderate resolution imaging spectroradiometer using convolutional neural networks,034520_1.pdf,C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\forlitrev_pdfs\034520_1.pdf,1,C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1\034520_1.tei.xml,1

    Where-Object { $_.pdf_exists -eq "0" } |
    Format-Table paper_id, doi_resolved, primary_file, title -AutoSize

    paper_id doi_resolved primary_file

    10_1007_s13201_021_01456_8 Assessment of heavy metal and physico‑chemical pollution loadings of River B...

    "10_1016_j_proenv_2011_09_268","","Research on the Water Quality Monitoring System for Inland.docx","C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\forlitrev_pdfs\Research on the Water Quality Monitoring System for Inland.docx","Research on the Water Quality Monitoring System for Inland Lakes based on Remote Sensing"
