    How do I get these papers? Many of them are scarce resources talking about the tropics which has little academic coverage.

    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    Excellent—this is exactly the outcome we wanted, and it tells us precisely what remains.

    What your numbers mean (no hand-waving)

    • DOI-based linking (after the fix): 241 / 268 included are now covered by TEI.
    • Stem-based linking via primary_file: 253 / 268 included have TEI available.
    • You have 267 / 268 PDFs present locally (so only 1 included record points to a missing/nonexistent PDF).
    • Therefore the “real” blockers are now very small and concrete:
      1. ~14 included papers where TEI exists but DOI extraction still doesn’t match the registry DOI (253–241 = 12, plus a couple edge conditions), and/or
      2. the 9 PDF failures (from your GROBID log), but only some of those may be in the included set, and
      3. the 1 missing PDF.

    So we can proceed to extraction using the stem-linked TEI path as the source of truth and treat DOI as metadata (not as the join key).

    Step 6 (next): create the “Included Evidence Pack v1” (a single table that drives everything)

    This is the table we will use for chunking + extraction. It will guarantee every included record has a TEI path if it exists.

    6.1 Build a final included→TEI table (merge DOI coverage + stem coverage)

    Create a new script: .\scripts\build_included_evidence_pack_manifest_v1.py

    What it will do:

    • read registry
    • read included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.csv
    • output a manifest with one row per included paper:
      • paper_id, doi_resolved, title, year_verified, primary_file, pdf_path, tei_path, tei_exists
    • choose TEI path by stem first (because it’s deterministic for your corpus), and optionally store doi_linked = 0/1

    Before I write the exact script, one quick reality check: your registry has year_verified and title_clean and other fields—we’ll include them.

    If you want to proceed immediately without extra back-and-forth, run this and paste the first 2 lines of the stem-link map so I can confirm columns (though I already know them from the script we just created):

    PowerShell
    Get-Content .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.csv -TotalCount 2

    (But we can also proceed without this; it’s just to avoid any mismatch due to Excel adding BOM, etc.)

    Step 7 (parallel cleanup): identify the exact remaining gaps (actionable list)

    You now have all the artifacts to isolate the remaining problem children.

    7.1 Find the 1 missing PDF (included but pdf_exists=0)

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.csv |
  Where-Object { $_.pdf_exists -eq "0" } |
  Format-Table paper_id, doi_resolved, primary_file, title -AutoSize

    That tells you which file to re-download/rename.

    7.2 Find included papers with PDF but no TEI by stem (these are your “true TEI gaps”)

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.csv |
  Where-Object { $_.pdf_exists -eq "1" -and $_.tei_exists -eq "0" } |
  Select-Object paper_id, doi_resolved, primary_file, pdf_path_resolved, title |
  Export-Csv -NoTypeInformation -Encoding utf8 .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_pdf_no_tei_v1.csv

(Get-Content .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_pdf_no_tei_v1.csv -TotalCount 10)

    Those will mostly overlap with your 9 GROBID failures, but this isolates only the ones that matter for included studies.

    7.3 Re-run GROBID just for those missing TEIs (targeted retry)

    Once you have included_pdf_no_tei_v1.csv, we can write a tiny retry script that reads that list and calls your existing GROBID endpoint for only those PDFs (so you don’t reprocess 375 again). If the failures are due to scanning, we’ll OCR only those.

    Step 8 (the real next milestone): TEI → section-aware chunks (Methods/Validation/Preprocessing)

    Now that you have 253 included TEIs, we should move to chunking.

    What “chunking” must do for your review RQs

    For each TEI, output chunks with:

    • paper_id
    • tei_path
    • section (best effort from TEI structure)
    • text
    • char_count
    • (optional) is_methods_like boolean based on section headings

    This enables:

    • evidence-backed extraction (validation design, sample size, atmospheric correction, matchup windows, uncertainty reporting)
    • transparent citations: you can store tei_path + section heading + snippet

    Your next action (no ambiguity)

    Run these two commands and paste the outputs:

    1. missing PDF row(s):
    PowerShell
    Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.csv |
  Where-Object { $_.pdf_exists -eq "0" } |
  Format-Table paper_id, doi_resolved, primary_file, title -AutoSize
    1. count of included PDF-without-TEI rows:
    PowerShell
    Import-Csv .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.csv |
  Where-Object { $_.pdf_exists -eq "1" -and $_.tei_exists -eq "0" } |
  Measure-Object

    With those, I’ll give you the exact next scripts:

    • a manifest builder (included→TEI) that becomes the single source of truth, and
    • a TEI chunker that outputs chunks.jsonl ready for extraction.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> python .\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py
    Wrote: C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\tei_coverage_report_v2.csv
    Included DOIs: 268
    Unique TEI DOIs: 332
    Included with TEI: 241
    Included missing TEI: 27
    Duplicate TEI DOIs: 82
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> code .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> python .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py
    Included rows: 268
    Have primary_file: 268
    PDF found: 267
    TEI found via stem: 253
    Wrote: C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.csv
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    Oguta review: CORE scripts (text-only)

    https://gist.github.com/Achionye/ca782340e7ab67d7a152c94c00717185

    Your “Included missing TEI: 55” is now mostly a linking/DOI-parsing issue, not a lack of TEI files. You proved you have TEIs (366 OK), but tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py is currently extracting DOIs in a way that can accidentally swallow the file extension (because it runs the DOI regex on p.name, and your DOI regex allows dots; then norm_doi() does not strip suffixes/punctuation). That’s exactly how a filename like 10.1371_journal.pntd.0009634.tei.xml can yield a “DOI” that looks like 10.1371/journal.pntd.0009634.tei.xml and therefore won’t match your registry DOI. 1

    Step 1 — Patch tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py (two tiny edits, high yield)

    Open: .\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py and change only these functions.

    Replace norm_doi() with this

    Python
    def norm_doi(d):
 d = clean(d).lower()
 d = d.replace("\u200b", "")  # zero-width space
 d = re.sub(r"^https?://(dx\.)?doi\.org/", "", d)
 d = re.sub(r"^doi:\s*", "", d)
 d = d.strip()

 # remove common wrappers / trailing punctuation
 d = d.strip(" <>[]{}()\"'.,;:")

 # strip file-ish suffixes that can leak in from filenames
 d = re.sub(r"\.tei(\.xml)?$", "", d)
 d = re.sub(r"\.xml$", "", d)
 d = re.sub(r"\.pdf$", "", d)

 return d

    Replace doi_from_filename() with this (strip extensions BEFORE regex)

    Python
    def doi_from_filename(name: str) -> str:
 # supports filenames like 10.5194_hess-24-535-2020.tei.xml
 base = re.sub(r"\.tei\.xml$", "", name, flags=re.I)
 base = re.sub(r"\.xml$", "", base, flags=re.I)
 m = DOI_RE.search(base.replace("_","/"))
 return norm_doi(m.group(1)) if m else ""

    Why this is the correct minimal fix (based on your script): your current doi_from_filename() runs on name.replace("_","/") where name still includes .tei.xml, and DOI_RE allows . so it can match through the extension; then norm_doi() doesn’t remove it. 1

    Re-run coverage

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py

    You should see Included with TEI increase and missing drop (often noticeably).

    Step 2 — Remove the DOI dependency ceiling using primary_file (your registry already supports this)

    Your registry header shows you already store primary_file per paper. That’s perfect: for TEIs generated from forlitrev_pdfs, the TEI filename is deterministically PDF_STEM + ".tei.xml" in .\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1\.

    So even if a TEI contains no DOI (or DOI parsing fails), you can still link included paper → PDF → TEI and proceed with extraction.

    Create this new script: .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    Copy/paste as-is:

    Python
    from __future__ import annotations
from pathlib import Path
import csv, re, os

ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
REG  = ROOT / "screening_v1" / "study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv"
PDFDIR = ROOT / "forlitrev_pdfs"
TEIDIR = ROOT / "fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1"
OUT = ROOT / "out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1" / "included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.csv"

def clean(s): return re.sub(r"\s+"," ",(s or "")).strip()

def resolve_pdf_path(primary_file: str) -> Path | None:
    if not primary_file:
        return None
    p = Path(primary_file)

    # absolute path
    if p.is_absolute():
        return p if p.exists() else None

    # relative to project root
    p1 = ROOT / p
    if p1.exists():
        return p1

    # relative to PDFDIR by basename
    p2 = PDFDIR / Path(primary_file).name
    if p2.exists():
        return p2

    return None

rows = list(csv.DictReader(open(REG, encoding="utf-8-sig", newline="")))
inc = [r for r in rows if clean(r.get("include_final","")) == "1"]

out_rows = []
have_primary = 0
pdf_found = 0
tei_found = 0

for r in inc:
    primary = clean(r.get("primary_file",""))
    if primary:
        have_primary += 1

    pdfp = resolve_pdf_path(primary)
    pdf_exists = int(pdfp.exists()) if pdfp else 0
    if pdf_exists:
        pdf_found += 1

    teip = None
    tei_exists = 0
    if pdfp:
        teip = TEIDIR / (pdfp.stem + ".tei.xml")
        tei_exists = int(teip.exists())
        if tei_exists:
            tei_found += 1

    out_rows.append({
        "paper_id": clean(r.get("paper_id","")),
        "doi_resolved": clean(r.get("doi_resolved","")),
        "title": clean(r.get("title","")),
        "primary_file": primary,
        "pdf_path_resolved": str(pdfp) if pdfp else "",
        "pdf_exists": pdf_exists,
        "tei_path_from_stem": str(teip) if teip else "",
        "tei_exists": tei_exists,
    })

OUT.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
with open(OUT, "w", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=list(out_rows[0].keys()))
    w.writeheader()
    w.writerows(out_rows)

print("Included rows:", len(inc))
print("Have primary_file:", have_primary)
print("PDF found:", pdf_found)
print("TEI found via stem:", tei_found)
print("Wrote:", OUT)

    Run it

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py

    This will tell us, for your 268 included papers, how many can be linked to TEI even without DOI extraction.

    Step 3 — Then we decide what the “55 missing” really are

    After:

    1. the DOI normalization patch re-run, and
    2. the primary_file → TEI linkmap run,

    you’ll know exactly which cases remain “missing” for real (typically:

    • included papers where primary_file is blank/not pointing to a local PDF,
    • the 9 failed PDFs (no TEI created),
    • or PDFs that exist but are named differently than the registry expects).

    At that point we can close the gap in a targeted way (rename/link, re-download, or OCR only the failures), and then move to the next major step: section-aware chunking + evidence-backed field extraction.

    If you paste the 4-line summary printed by included_primaryfile_to_tei_map_v1.py after you run it, I’ll tell you the exact next step with no guessing.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    "Wrote: C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\tei_coverage_report_v2.csv
    Included DOIs: 268
    Unique TEI DOIs: 332
    Included with TEI: 213
    Included missing TEI: 55
    Duplicate TEI DOIs: 82
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> Get-Content .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_missing_tei_v2.txt | Select-Object -First 25
    10.1007/s10661-023-11874-7
    10.1007/s11356-020-11765-1
    10.1007/s11356-022-23431-9
    10.1007/s11356-023-26878-6
    10.1007/s42452-018-0027-6
    10.1016/j.earscirev.2020.103187
    10.1016/j.proenv.2011.09.268
    10.1038/s41598-024-66699-2

    10.1364/oe.397456

    10.15625/0866-7187/39/3/10270

    10.17159/sajs.2018/4841

    10.20944/preprints202309.0489.v1
    10.2166/hydro.2022.151
    10.2166/wpt.2022.046
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> (Get-Content .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_missing_tei_v2.txt | Measure-Object).Count
    55
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> (Get-Content .\screening_v1\study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv -TotalCount 1)
    paper_id,bucket,include_final,reason_excluded,doc_type,relevance_rank,scope_bucket,title,year_raw,doi_raw,journal_raw,year_suspicious,sensors,parameters,ac_methods,ml_models,gis_tools,metrics,validation_n,qc_flags,primary_file,sections_markdown,title_clean,year_resolved,doi_resolved,venue_resolved,meta_source,meta_confidence,needs_manual_bib_check,notes,year_raw__orig,sync_status,venue,year_verified,year_verified_source,year_verified_note,doi_raw__orig,doi_override,doi_override_source,year_raw_legacy,year_verified__orig
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>"

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    Oguta review: CORE scripts (text-only)

    https://gist.github.com/Achionye/ca782340e7ab67d7a152c94c00717185

    You’ve just crossed the hardest milestone: GROBID full‑text TEI is now available for 366/375 PDFs, and your DOI-based coverage jumped from 5 → 213 included papers with TEI. That’s excellent.

    Now the work splits cleanly into two parallel “unblockers”:

    1. fix the 55 “missing TEI” cases (mostly a linking problem, not a “no TEI exists” problem), and
    2. deal with the 9 failed PDFs (true extraction failures).

    Below is the exact next step sequence tailored to your current outputs and your scripts.

    Step 5A — First, inspect what the 55 “missing TEI” actually are (quick, deterministic)

    Your script already wrote them here:

    • .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_missing_tei_v2.txt 1

    Run:

    PowerShell
    Get-Content .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_missing_tei_v2.txt | Select-Object -First 25
(Get-Content .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_missing_tei_v2.txt | Measure-Object).Count

    This confirms the 55 list is stable and gives us examples.

    Why this matters: right now your coverage logic keys on DOI extracted from TEI (either from filename or anywhere in text). 1 If a TEI contains a DOI with trailing punctuation (common in references) your current norm_doi() won’t strip it, so it won’t match your registry DOI.

    Step 5B — High-yield fix: improve DOI normalization (this often recovers a chunk of the 55)

    In scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py, your norm_doi() currently only lowercases + removes a doi.org prefix. 1 That is often too weak because your DOI regex explicitly allows characters like ) ; . etc. 1

    Edit this function in tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py

    Replace:

    Python
    def norm_doi(d):
 d=clean(d).lower()
 d=re.sub(r"^https?://(dx\.)?doi\.org/","",d)
 return d

    with this stronger, end-trimming version:

    Python
    def norm_doi(d):
 d = clean(d).lower()
 d = d.replace("\u200b","")  # zero-width space
 d = re.sub(r"^https?://(dx\.)?doi\.org/","",d)
 d = re.sub(r"^doi:\s*","",d)
 d = d.strip()

 # Strip common wrapping/bracketing and trailing punctuation
 d = d.strip(" <>[]{}()\"'.,;:")
 d = re.sub(r"[.,;:]+$", "", d)   # extra safety
 return d

    Then re-run:

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py

    Expected result: Included with TEI increases and “missing 55” shrinks (sometimes dramatically) because many TEIs contain the DOI but with punctuation artifacts.

    Step 5C — Make the project “not limited”: add a fallback linker that does NOT depend on DOI existing in TEI

    Even with better normalization, some TEIs genuinely won’t contain the DOI anywhere (publisher layout, DOI only on a cover page, or stripped). DOI-only linking will always leave some behind.

    So we add one small new step: link included papers to TEI by PDF stem (and title if needed).

    Why PDF-stem linking is reliable in your pipeline

    Your batch extractor writes TEIs as:

    outdir / (p.stem + ".tei.xml") 1

    So for the PDFs that succeeded, there is a deterministic TEI path by stem—no DOI required.

    Create a new script: scripts/link_included_to_tei_by_pdfstem_v1.py

    This script will:

    • read study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv
    • for each included record, try to locate its PDF (if your registry has a pdf/pdf_name/file column; if not, it will fall back to “search by DOI-in-filename” and finally “search by title keyword”)
    • then map to fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1\<pdf_stem>.tei.xml
    • output out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_tei_linkmap_v1.csv

    I’m not going to guess your registry’s PDF filename column name without seeing it. So do this one command and paste the header row here:

    PowerShell
    (Get-Content .\screening_v1\study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv -TotalCount 1)

    Then I’ll give you the exact script body with the correct column names, so this becomes plug-and-play.

    (We can proceed even without this fallback right now because you already have 213/268 linked by DOI, but this is what removes the “limited by DOI-in-TEI” ceiling permanently.)

    Step 6 — Quarantine and salvage the 9 failed PDFs (so TEI OK becomes ~375/375)

    Your log already contains the 9 failures. Pull them out:

    PowerShell
    $log = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log.csv"
Import-Csv $log | Where-Object status -eq "failed" | Select-Object pdf, error

    Typical causes:

    • encrypted/password PDF
    • corrupted download
    • scanned image PDF (less common for GROBID to “hard fail,” but it happens)
    • very large/odd PDFs that time out

    Fastest salvage path (only for those 9):

    1. re-download (if they’re corrupted) OR
    2. OCR them and re-run only those PDFs

    If you want OCR, the practical tool is ocrmypdf (plus Tesseract). We’ll only deploy OCR after we see what the 9 are, because many failures are just “bad PDF” and OCR won’t help.

    Step 7 — Don’t wait: you can start “evidence pack” extraction now for the 213

    You already have enough coverage to start building your review tables (RQ1–RQ7) while we close the remaining gap. This is exactly how to keep momentum without sacrificing rigor.

    Next two things to paste (and I’ll give you the next exact commands/scripts)

    1. CSV header row:
    PowerShell
    (Get-Content .\screening_v1\study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv -TotalCount 1)
    1. The 9 failed entries:
    PowerShell
    $log = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log.csv"
Import-Csv $log | Where-Object status -eq "failed" | Select-Object pdf, error

    With those, I’ll tailor the linker script to your registry schema and tell you precisely whether OCR is worth it for the failures.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    DONE
    TEI OK: 366 of 375
    Out dir: fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1
    Log: .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log.csv
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> Import-Csv $log | Group-Object status | Sort-Object Count -Descending | Format-Table Count, Name

    Count Name

    366 ok
    9 failed

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> python .\scripts\find_grobid_outputs_v1.py
    Found TEI-like files: 484
    Wrote: C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_inventory.csv
    Top 10 by size:
    fulltext_tei 560246 fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1\environments-10-00170-v2.tei.xml
    fulltext_tei 489922 fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1\IMPROVING REMOTE SENSING ALGORITHMS TOWARDS INLAND WATER.tei.xml
    fulltext_tei 481031 fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1\remotesensing-13-04347-v3.tei.xml
    fulltext_tei 415200 fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1\Remote sensing of laboratory rivers.tei.xml
    fulltext_tei 398486 fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1\Integrating Inland and Coastal Water Quality Data for Actionable Knowledge.tei.xml
    fulltext_tei 398486 openalex_expansion\grobid_tei_missing_sections\Integrating Inland and Coastal Water Quality Data for Actionable Knowledge.tei.xml
    fulltext_tei 359124 fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1\Research Trends in the Use of Remote Sensing forInland Water Quality Science Moving TowardsMultidisciplinary Applications.tei.xml
    fulltext_tei 355870 fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1\Towards Synoptic Water Monitoring Systems A Review of AI Methods for Automating Water Body Detection and Water Quality Monitoring Using Remote Sensing.tei.xml
    fulltext_tei 340634 fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1\Remote_Sensing_for_Monitoring_Surface_Wa.tei.xml
    fulltext_tei 331141 fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1\Lake Water Quality Management in.tei.xml
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> python .\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py
    Wrote: C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\tei_coverage_report_v2.csv
    Included DOIs: 268
    Unique TEI DOIs: 332
    Included with TEI: 213
    Included missing TEI: 55
    Duplicate TEI DOIs: 82
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>

    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    You’re already basically done: GROBID is running right now as a container named grobid and it’s already publishing 8070 (and 8071). Your only mistake was this line:

    PowerShell
    docker run ... f0bf986225c6

    f0bf986225c6 is a container ID, not an image name. docker run expects an image (e.g., grobid/grobid:0.8.2.1-crf), so Docker tried (and failed) to “pull” an image called f0bf986225c6:latest.

    What to do now (minimal steps)

    1) Confirm the running container is actually serving the API your scripts call

    PowerShell
    Invoke-WebRequest "http://127.0.0.1:8070/api/isalive" -UseBasicParsing -TimeoutSec 10 |
  Select-Object StatusCode, Content

    If you get 200 and true, you are unblocked. Do not start a new container.

    2) Run your TEI batch extraction (now that the server is up)

    PowerShell
    cd C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project

$teiOut = ".\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1"
$log    = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log.csv"
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force $teiOut | Out-Null

python .\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py `
  --pdfdir .\forlitrev_pdfs `
  --outdir $teiOut `
  --log $log `
  --retries 3

    3) Summarize outcomes

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv $log | Group-Object status | Sort-Object Count -Descending | Format-Table Count, Name

    4) Recompute TEI coverage vs included (your earlier metrics should jump)

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\find_grobid_outputs_v1.py
python .\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py

    Only if /api/isalive fails: restart GROBID correctly (using the image name)

    Because you already loaded the image, the correct restart is:

    PowerShell
    docker stop grobid
docker rm grobid

docker run --rm --init --name grobid -p 8070:8070 -p 8071:8071 grobid/grobid:0.8.2.1-crf

    Then re-run the /api/isalive check.

    Paste the output of the /api/isalive command and the Group-Object status table from the batch log, and I’ll tell you the exact next fix (OCR gate vs DOI-linking vs retry strategy) based on what your run actually produced.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> docker ps
    CONTAINER ID IMAGE COMMAND CREATED STATUS PORTS NAMES
    f0bf986225c6 grobid/grobid:0.8.2.1-crf "/tini -s -- ./grobi…" 6 days ago Up About a minute 0.0.0.0:8070-8071->8070-8071/tcp, [::]:8070-8071->8070-8071/tcp grobid
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> docker load -i "C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\grobid_0.8.2.1-crf.tar"
    Loaded image: grobid/grobid:0.8.2.1-crf
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> docker images | findstr /i grobid
    WARNING: This output is designed for human readability. For machine-readable output, please use --format.
    grobid/grobid:0.8.2.1-crf ede61849550e 1.46GB 410MB U
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> docker run --rm --init -p 8070:8070 f0bf986225c6
    Unable to find image 'f0bf986225c6:latest' locally
    docker: Error response from daemon: pull access denied for f0bf986225c6, repository does not exist or may require 'docker login'

    Run 'docker run --help' for more information
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>

    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    com.docker.service is now running, but that is not the Linux Docker Engine your CLI context (desktop-linux) is trying to talk to. The error

    open //./pipe/dockerDesktopLinuxEngine: The system cannot find the file specified

    means the Linux engine pipe hasn’t been created, which almost always means: Docker Desktop (the app/backend) isn’t started yet, or WSL2 backend isn’t healthy.

    Here’s the exact, minimal path forward on your machine.

    1) Start Docker Desktop application (this is what brings up the Linux engine)

    Run this from PowerShell:

    PowerShell
    $dd1 = "$Env:ProgramFiles\Docker\Docker\Docker Desktop.exe"
$dd2 = "$Env:LocalAppData\Programs\Docker\Docker\Docker Desktop.exe"

if (Test-Path $dd1) { Start-Process $dd1 }
elseif (Test-Path $dd2) { Start-Process $dd2 }
else { throw "Docker Desktop.exe not found." }

    Then wait 30–90 seconds (first start can be slow).

    Verify it’s actually running:

    PowerShell
    Get-Process -Name "Docker Desktop" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Get-Process -Name "com.docker.backend","com.docker.service" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

    Now try:

    PowerShell
    docker ps

    If docker ps works, you’re unblocked—skip to section 4.

    2) If Docker Desktop is running but docker ps still fails: check WSL2 status

    Docker Desktop’s Linux engine uses WSL2. Check:

    PowerShell
    wsl --status
wsl -l -v

    What you want to see:

    • WSL is installed
    • your default version is 2
    • at least one distro exists (Ubuntu, etc.)

    If WSL isn’t installed / not version 2, you’ll need the standard enable/install + reboot path:

    PowerShell
    wsl --install

    (That usually requires admin rights and a reboot.)

    Also try a quick backend reset (very commonly fixes the missing pipe):

    PowerShell
    wsl --shutdown

    Then re-open Docker Desktop and try docker ps again.

    3) If you need a “hard reset” of Docker Desktop backend from CLI

    Sometimes the backend gets stuck. Do:

    PowerShell
    Stop-Process -Name "Docker Desktop" -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Stop-Process -Name "com.docker.backend" -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
wsl --shutdown
Start-Process "$Env:ProgramFiles\Docker\Docker\Docker Desktop.exe"

    Wait again, then:

    PowerShell
    docker ps

    4) Once docker ps works: load your offline GROBID image tar + run it

    Now your grobid_0.8.2.1-crf.tar becomes useful.

    4A) Load image

    PowerShell
    docker load -i "C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\grobid_0.8.2.1-crf.tar"
docker images | findstr /i grobid

    4B) Run container (replace the image name/tag with what you see from docker images)

    PowerShell
    docker run --rm --init -p 8070:8070 <IMAGE_NAME:TAG>

    4C) Confirm GROBID HTTP is alive (this is what your Python scripts require)

    PowerShell
    Invoke-WebRequest "http://127.0.0.1:8070/api/isalive" -UseBasicParsing -TimeoutSec 5 |
  Select-Object StatusCode, Content

    When that returns 200 + true, you can run your TEI batch extraction.

    The one output I need from you to choose the next branch precisely

    After you do section 1, paste:

    1. Get-Process -Name "Docker Desktop","com.docker.backend" ... output, and
    2. wsl -l -v output (only if docker ps still fails).

    With those two, I can tell you exactly which failure mode you’re in (Docker Desktop not started vs WSL2 missing vs backend stuck) and the minimal fix.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> Get-Service com.docker.service -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Format-Table Status, Name, StartType

    Status Name StartType

    Stopped com.docker.service Manual

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> # If it exists but isn't running:
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> Start-Service com.docker.service
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> Get-Service com.docker.service -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Format-Table Status, Name, StartType

    Status Name StartType

    Running com.docker.service Manual

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> # If it exists but isn't running:
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> Start-Service com.docker.service
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> docker context show
    desktop-linux
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> docker ps
    failed to connect to the docker API at npipe:////./pipe/dockerDesktopLinuxEngine; check if the path is correct and if the daemon is running: open //./pipe/dockerDesktopLinuxEngine: The system cannot find the file specified.
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>

    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    What you have right now is the Docker client (docker.exe) but not the Docker daemon/engine. That’s why docker version prints the client info and then fails with:

    failed to connect to the docker API ... dockerDesktopLinuxEngine

    Your grobid_0.8.2.1-crf.tar is almost certainly a Docker image tarball (an offline/exported image). It’s not a Windows executable GROBID server by itself. You can use it only after the Docker engine is running, via docker load -i ....

    So the immediate goal is: start a GROBID HTTP server at http://127.0.0.1:8070 (because your scripts call that endpoint).

    Step 1 — Start Docker Desktop properly (PowerShell, no guessing)

    1A) Check if the Docker Desktop Windows service exists and start it

    PowerShell
    Get-Service com.docker.service -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Format-Table Status, Name, StartType

# If it exists but isn't running:
Start-Service com.docker.service

    If Get-Service com.docker.service returns nothing, Docker Desktop may not be installed (or installed per-user in a way that didn’t register the service).

    1B) Start Docker Desktop GUI (this is what brings up the Linux engine)

    PowerShell
    $dd1 = "$Env:ProgramFiles\Docker\Docker\Docker Desktop.exe"
$dd2 = "$Env:LocalAppData\Programs\Docker\Docker\Docker Desktop.exe"

if (Test-Path $dd1) { Start-Process $dd1 }
elseif (Test-Path $dd2) { Start-Process $dd2 }
else { "Docker Desktop.exe not found in common locations." }

    Then wait until Docker Desktop shows “Running”.

    1C) Confirm the daemon is reachable

    PowerShell
    docker context show
docker ps

    Only proceed when docker ps works.

    Step 2 — Load your GROBID image tar and run it (uses your existing .tar)

    Once Docker is running:

    2A) Load the image from the tarball

    PowerShell
    docker load -i "C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\grobid_0.8.2.1-crf.tar"

    Then list images and look for grobid:

    PowerShell
    docker images | Select-String -Pattern "grobid" -SimpleMatch

    2B) Run the container on port 8070

    Replace <IMAGE_NAME:TAG> with whatever name/tag appears after docker load (Docker prints it), for example grobid/grobid:0.8.2-crf:

    PowerShell
    docker run --rm --init -p 8070:8070 <IMAGE_NAME:TAG>

    Leave this terminal open (the server is running).

    2C) Confirm GROBID is alive (this is what your Python scripts need)

    PowerShell
    Invoke-WebRequest "http://127.0.0.1:8070/api/isalive" -UseBasicParsing -TimeoutSec 5 |
  Select-Object StatusCode, Content

    You want StatusCode = 200 and Content containing true.

    Step 3 — Now rerun your TEI generation batch (your existing plan)

    Once /api/isalive is good:

    PowerShell
    $teiOut = ".\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1"
$log    = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log.csv"

New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force $teiOut | Out-Null

python .\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py `
  --pdfdir .\forlitrev_pdfs `
  --outdir $teiOut `
  --log $log `
  --retries 3

    Why I’m not telling you to “run grobid locally without Docker” from that .tar

    Because that .tar is an image export. It won’t start a server on Windows by itself. Without Docker (or another container runtime), it’s just a file on disk.

    If Docker Desktop won’t start (common corporate blocker)

    Then we switch to a non-Docker deployment path (WSL2 running GROBID, or building/running GROBID from source with Java). But the fastest path given your current assets is the Docker Desktop engine + docker load + docker run, and it directly matches how your scripts are written.

    Run Step 1A–1C and paste the output of:

    • Get-Service com.docker.service ...
    • docker ps

    Then I’ll tell you exactly which branch you’re on (daemon not installed vs daemon not running vs context mismatch) and the next command to fix it.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> docker version
    Client:
    Version: 29.2.1
    API version: 1.53
    Go version: go1.25.6
    Git commit: a5c7197
    Built: Mon Feb 2 17:20:16 2026
    OS/Arch: windows/amd64
    Context: desktop-linux
    failed to connect to the docker API at npipe:////./pipe/dockerDesktopLinuxEngine; check if the path is correct and if the daemon is running: open //./pipe/dockerDesktopLinuxEngine: The system cannot find the file specified.
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> docker ps
    failed to connect to the docker API at npipe:////./pipe/dockerDesktopLinuxEngine; check if the path is correct and if the daemon is running: open //./pipe/dockerDesktopLinuxEngine: The system cannot find the file specified.
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>

    Grobid exists here: "C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\grobid_0.8.2.1-crf.tar"

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    Using GROBID | kermitt2/grobid | DeepWiki

    https://deepwiki.com/kermitt2/grobid/3-installation-and-deployment

    2

    GROBID with containers - GROBID Documentation

    https://grobid.readthedocs.io/en/latest/Grobid-docker/

    3

    GitHub - grobidOrg/grobid-client-python: Python client for GROBID Web services

    https://github.com/kermitt2/grobid_client_python

    That docker ps error means there is no Docker daemon running on your machine right now, so there is no way a Docker-based GROBID server could be running (at least not via Docker Desktop). Until a GROBID HTTP service is running at http://127.0.0.1:8070, your scripts that call /api/processFulltextDocument will fail. 1

    Step 1 — Start the thing your “local GROBID” depends on (Docker Desktop)

    1. Launch Docker Desktop from the Start menu and wait until it says it’s running.
    2. Then verify the daemon is up:
    PowerShell
    docker version
docker ps

    If docker ps still gives the same dockerDesktopLinuxEngine pipe error, Docker Desktop is not running (or not installed / WSL2 backend not enabled).

    Step 2 — Start a GROBID container (this actually creates the HTTP server your scripts need)

    The official docs show running GROBID on port 8070 with Docker like this (CRF-only image is fine for TEI extraction): 2

    PowerShell
    docker pull grobid/grobid:0.8.2-crf

docker run --rm --init --ulimit core=0 -p 8070:8070 grobid/grobid:0.8.2-crf

    Leave that container running in its terminal.

    (Equivalent image name also exists under lfoppiano/grobid, and many examples use it; both are widely referenced. 2)

    Step 3 — Confirm the exact health endpoint your scripts rely on

    GROBID exposes:

    • GET http://localhost:8070/api/isalive → should return true when ready 1

    Run:

    PowerShell
    Invoke-WebRequest "http://127.0.0.1:8070/api/isalive" -UseBasicParsing | Select-Object StatusCode, Content

    Only when that works should you start the batch TEI build.

    Step 4 — Run your batch TEI generation (your existing plan)

    Once /api/isalive returns true, proceed with:

    PowerShell
    $teiOut = ".\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1"
$log    = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log.csv"
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force $teiOut | Out-Null

python .\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py `
  --pdfdir .\forlitrev_pdfs `
  --outdir $teiOut `
  --log $log `
  --retries 3

    Why this matters for your current coverage numbers

    Right now you have 268 included DOIs but only 5 included-with-TEI, which is exactly what you’d see if the included PDFs haven’t yet been run through a working GROBID service in this project (or if the service wasn’t running at the time). Getting the service up is the gating step.

    If Docker Desktop cannot be used in your environment (policy/permissions), the practical fallback is to run GROBID in a Linux environment (e.g., WSL2) or use a hosted instance—but hosted instances usually have throughput/usage limits and are a bad fit for 375 PDFs. 3

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> docker ps
    failed to connect to the docker API at npipe:////./pipe/dockerDesktopLinuxEngine; check if the path is correct and if the daemon is running: open //./pipe/dockerDesktopLinuxEngine: The system cannot find the file specified.
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>

    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    Because your scripts don’t talk to “a local executable called grobid” directly—they talk to a GROBID server over HTTP.

    In your codebase, both:

    • grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py, and
    • grobid_retry_missing_tei_v1.py

    send PDFs to an endpoint like:

    • http://127.0.0.1:8070/api/processFulltextDocument

    So “having GROBID locally” only helps if the server is actually running and listening on port 8070 at the moment you start the batch. The quick Invoke-WebRequest http://127.0.0.1:8070 check is just a 2‑second guard that prevents you from launching a long batch run that would otherwise fail 375 times with connection errors.

    A more targeted check (hits the exact API route your scripts use)

    Run this instead (it’s closer to your reality than the root URL):

    PowerShell
    try {
  $r = Invoke-WebRequest "http://127.0.0.1:8070/api/isalive" -UseBasicParsing -TimeoutSec 5
  $r.StatusCode
  $r.Content
} catch {
  $_.Exception.Message
}
    • If it returns 200 (and usually some text like “true”), you’re good.
    • If it times out / connection refused, then the server isn’t running on that port (common if Docker isn’t started, port changed, or it’s bound to another interface).

    If you run GROBID as Docker (common), the “real” check is:

    PowerShell
    docker ps

    Bottom line: this check isn’t generic busywork—it’s specifically aligned to how your Python batch scripts communicate with GROBID.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> cd C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> (Get-ChildItem .\forlitrev_pdfs -File -Filter *.pdf | Measure-Object).Count
    375
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> # This should return something (even an error page is fine); failure to connect means GROBID isn't running.
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>

    Why run "# This should return something (even an error page is fine); failure to connect means GROBID isn't running.
    Invoke-WebRequest "http://127.0.0.1:8070" -UseBasicParsing | Select-Object -First 1 StatusCode, StatusDescription" when I have grouid locally?

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    Oguta review: CORE scripts (text-only)

    https://gist.github.com/Achionye/ca782340e7ab67d7a152c94c00717185

    Those numbers tell us something very specific (and it’s not “your scripts are broken”):

    • tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py only counts a TEI as “covering” an included study if it can extract a DOI from either (a) the TEI filename (it tries name.replace("_","/") and then DOI-regex) or (b) inside the TEI text (<idno type="doi">…</idno> or any DOI regex match). 1
    • Right now, across your whole project, you only have 76 TEIs with extractable DOIs, and only 5 of those DOIs are in your 268 included DOIs. So the “missing TEI” count is huge mainly because you do not yet have TEI fulltexts generated (or DOI-identifiable) for your included PDF corpus—the TEIs you found are mostly from other folders (e.g., openalex_expansion\tei_trimmed\...) and/or TEIs where DOI isn’t present/recognized. 1

    So the next step is not “tweak coverage math”; it’s: generate a canonical TEI set from the PDFs you actually have in forlitrev_pdfs\, into a single folder inside your project root so it’s picked up by find_grobid_outputs_v1.py. 1

    Step 3 (next command you run): Batch-build TEI for your PDFs (canonical output)

    3.0 Quick reality check: how many PDFs do you have locally?

    PowerShell
    cd C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project
(Get-ChildItem .\forlitrev_pdfs -File -Filter *.pdf | Measure-Object).Count

    3.1 Confirm GROBID is reachable (otherwise the batch will fail)

    PowerShell
    # This should return something (even an error page is fine); failure to connect means GROBID isn't running.
Invoke-WebRequest "http://127.0.0.1:8070" -UseBasicParsing | Select-Object -First 1 StatusCode, StatusDescription

    3.2 Run your existing robust batch script (v3 retry)

    This is your script that already does: skip-if-existing, retries, and writes a CSV log. 1

    PowerShell
    $teiOut = ".\fulltext_tei_from_pdfs_v1"
$log    = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_from_pdfs_v1_log.csv"

New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force $teiOut | Out-Null

python .\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py `
  --pdfdir .\forlitrev_pdfs `
  --outdir $teiOut `
  --log $log `
  --retries 3

    3.3 Summarize the log immediately (so we see if OCR is needed)

    PowerShell
    Import-Csv $log | Group-Object status | Sort-Object Count -Descending | Format-Table Count, Name

    What you want to see:

    • lots of ok (good)
    • some skipped_exists (fine if re-running)
    • if you see many failed or you later see many “empty_or_small_tei” in console/log, that’s where OCR becomes necessary for scanned PDFs.

    Step 4 (verify improvement): re-inventory and recompute coverage

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\find_grobid_outputs_v1.py
python .\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py

    Expected change:

    • Found TEI-like files should jump by roughly “#PDFs successfully processed”
    • Unique TEI DOIs should increase (depending on how often DOI is present in TEI header/body)
    • Included with TEI should increase substantially if your PDFs are indeed the included corpus

    If “Included with TEI” is still low after the batch

    That means GROBID is producing TEIs but DOI extraction is failing (common when DOI isn’t present on first pages, or publisher formatting is odd). In that case, we don’t stop—we switch the join key from DOI-only to a secondary key your pipeline already uses: title normalization / paper_id, and we’ll add a small “TEI↔registry linker” step (title-based fallback) so extraction is never blocked by missing DOI.

    But don’t do that yet—first run the batch + rerun coverage.

    Your next paste-back (so I can give the exact next step without guessing)

    After you run the batch, paste:

    1. the Group-Object status table, and
    2. the new Included DOIs / Unique TEI DOIs / Included with TEI / Included missing TEI lines.

    Then we’ll decide precisely whether you need OCR next, or whether we move straight to TEI→chunking→evidence-backed field extraction.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    Included DOIs: 268
    Unique TEI DOIs: 76
    Included with TEI: 5
    Included missing TEI: 263
    Duplicate TEI DOIs: 35
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    Oguta review: CORE scripts (text-only)

    https://gist.github.com/Achionye/ca782340e7ab67d7a152c94c00717185

    118 is consistent with what your current detector is actually counting, and it’s not necessarily “wrong”—it’s “TEI-like XML files that match a tight filename filter.” In find_grobid_outputs_v1.py, you only count files that (a) end with .tei.xml or end with .xml and have "tei" in the filename, and (b) contain <TEI or teiHeader in the first 5000 characters. 1 That means you will miss any valid GROBID TEI saved as plain .xml whose name does not contain tei (e.g., fulltext.xml, grobid.xml, etc.), and you will also see “smaller than expected” counts if many PDFs simply haven’t been processed into TEI inside this project root yet (or were processed into a folder outside oguta_review_project, which your script won’t scan). 1

    Step-by-step: what to do next (fully aligned to your scripts)

    Step 2 (do this now): compute coverage vs included (this tells us whether you’re truly missing TEIs)

    Run:

    PowerShell
    cd C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project
python .\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py

    This script will:

    • read your TEI inventory (out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_inventory.csv)
    • read your included set (screening_v1\study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv, include_final == "1")
    • dedupe TEIs by DOI (keeps the largest TEI per DOI)
    • print the key numbers: Included DOIs, Unique TEI DOIs, Included with TEI, Included missing TEI. 1

    Those printed counts are the real decision point for whether we proceed to chunking/extraction or we need to generate more TEIs.

    If Step 2 shows “Included missing TEI” is large: generate TEIs in one canonical folder (your fastest gap-fill)

    Use your own robust batch runner grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py (it retries, skips existing TEI > 5000 bytes, and logs outcomes). 1

    1. Make a single canonical TEI output directory:
    PowerShell
    $teiOut = ".\fulltext_tei_canonical"
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force $teiOut | Out-Null
$log = ".\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_batch_log.csv"
    1. Run the batch TEI build:
    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py `
  --pdfdir .\forlitrev_pdfs `
  --outdir $teiOut `
  --log $log `
  --retries 3
    1. Re-run inventory + coverage:
    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\find_grobid_outputs_v1.py
python .\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py

    That should push your TEI-like count toward your PDF count (minus genuinely failed PDFs, scanned PDFs, corrupt PDFs, etc.).

    If Step 2 looks too low even though you believe TEIs exist: fix the filename filter (your script is currently conservative)

    Right now you only consider .xml files if "tei" is in the filename. 1 If any TEIs are named without “tei” (common in some pipelines), they won’t be counted.

    Minimal safe change in find_grobid_outputs_v1.py (no redesign):

    • change this:
    Python
    if name.endswith(".tei.xml") or (name.endswith(".xml") and "tei" in name):
    • to this (keeps it cheap by using a size guard):
    Python
    if name.endswith(".tei.xml") or name.endswith(".xml"):
    if p.stat().st_size < 2000:
        continue

    Then your existing looks_like_tei() check still prevents most false positives. 1

    One tiny PowerShell “reality check” (optional but very informative)

    To see whether the issue is “missing TEIs” vs “TEIs exist but aren’t detected,” run these two counts:

    PowerShell
    # PDFs you actually have locally
(Get-ChildItem .\forlitrev_pdfs -File -Filter *.pdf | Measure-Object).Count

# TEIs matching your current strict naming convention
(Get-ChildItem -Recurse -File -Filter *.tei.xml | Measure-Object).Count

    If PDFs ≫ TEIs, you simply haven’t produced TEIs for most PDFs yet (or they failed); then the batch GROBID run above is the right next move.

    Your next action

    Run python .\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py and paste the 5 summary lines it prints (“Included DOIs… Included missing TEI…”). That single output will let me give you the exact next step (batch build vs detector tweak vs OCR gate) without guessing.

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    Oguta review: CORE scripts (text-only)

    https://gist.github.com/Achionye/ca782340e7ab67d7a152c94c00717185

    Now that I can see your real “core” scripts, we can proceed using your existing plumbing (not a redesign). The fastest, most defensible next step is to (1) inventory what fulltext artifacts you already have, (2) compute coverage against your included set, (3) fill the gaps with GROBID, then (4) re-check coverage.

    Step 1 — Build TEI inventory (what TEI files exist anywhere in the project)

    From project root:

    PowerShell
    cd C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project
python .\scripts\find_grobid_outputs_v1.py

    What it does (per your script): recursively scans your whole project for *.tei.xml / TEI-like XML and writes an inventory to .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_inventory.csv. 1

    Step 2 — Compute TEI coverage vs your included studies

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py

    What it does (per your script): reads

    • .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\grobid_inventory.csv (from Step 1),
    • .\screening_v1\study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING.csv (and uses include_final == "1"),

    then writes:

    • .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\tei_coverage_report_v2.csv
    • .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\tei_bestmap_v2.csv (largest TEI per DOI)
    • .\out_synthesis_filtered_full_v1\included_missing_tei_v2.txt (DOIs missing TEI) 1

    Step 3 — Fill missing TEIs (GROBID retry pass)

    This is your “make sure every PDF has a TEI” tool:

    PowerShell
    # choose ONE consistent TEI output folder for all runs
$teiDir = ".\grobid_tei_fulltext"
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force $teiDir | Out-Null

python .\scripts\grobid_retry_missing_tei_v1.py `
  --pdfdir .\forlitrev_pdfs `
  --teidir $teiDir

    Notes that matter for your implementation:

    • It decides “missing” by filename stem match (p.stem) between PDF and TEI files in --teidir, and writes p.stem + ".tei.xml" on success. 1
    • It hits http://127.0.0.1:8070/api/processFulltextDocument by default (or GROBID_URL env var). 1

    Step 4 — Re-run Steps 1–2 to confirm gaps are closed

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\find_grobid_outputs_v1.py
python .\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py

    At this point, you will have a quantified, auditable statement of “included studies with TEI available” and a concrete list of what’s still missing (if anything).

    What I need from you to continue Step-by-step (no guessing)

    Paste the console output from Step 2 (it prints counts like “Included DOIs”, “Included missing TEI”, etc.). Your script prints those summary lines at the end. 1

    Once I see that, the next step will be customized to your numbers:

    • if “missing TEI” is near zero → we move straight to chunking/indexing + evidence-backed field extraction
    • if “missing TEI” is high → we decide whether the bottleneck is GROBID server, PDF naming/stem mismatch, or scanned PDFs needing OCR (and we add only the minimal new tool for that, typically OCRmyPDF).

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    • Creating gist with multiple files
      ✓ Created secret gist RUN_CONTEXT.md
      https://gist.github.com/Achionye/ca782340e7ab67d7a152c94c00717185
      PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>
    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    You ran that command from ...\_gist_share\gist_repo, so the paths like .\scripts\litreview_pipeline_pc_v2_2_0.py don’t exist there (they exist in your project root). gh gist create checks that the file paths exist, and it aborts with “no matches found” when they don’t.

    Run this from your project root and (better) let PowerShell automatically pick the latest matching versions from your scripts\ folder (so we don’t guess exact filenames).

    Do this exactly (PowerShell)

    PowerShell
    # 1) Go to project root (not the gist repo)
cd C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project

# 2) Helper: pick latest file by pattern from .\scripts
function Pick-LatestScript([string]$pattern) {
  $f = Get-ChildItem .\scripts -File -Filter $pattern | Sort-Object LastWriteTime -Descending | Select-Object -First 1
  if (-not $f) { throw "Missing script pattern in .\scripts\: $pattern" }
  return $f.FullName
}

# 3) Build CORE list (latest versions)
$core = @(
  (Pick-LatestScript "litreview_pipeline_pc_v*.py"),
  (Pick-LatestScript "build_litreview_pipeline_v*.py"),

  (Pick-LatestScript "tei_coverage_vs_included_v*.py"),
  (Pick-LatestScript "find_grobid_outputs_v*.py"),
  (Pick-LatestScript "grobid_batch_process_cli_v*.py"),
  (Pick-LatestScript "grobid_retry_missing_tei_v*.py"),

  (Pick-LatestScript "json_md_coverage_vs_included_v*.py"),
  (Pick-LatestScript "md_section_coverage_included_v*.py"),
  (Pick-LatestScript "auto_extraction_discrepancy_scan_v*.py"),
  (Pick-LatestScript "build_extraction_audit_pack_v*.py")
)

# 4) Confirm what will be uploaded (so you see the exact files)
$core

# 5) Create the secret gist (secret is default; do NOT add --public)
gh gist create $core .\_gist_share\RUN_CONTEXT.md -d "Oguta review: CORE scripts (text-only)"

    That will print a new gist URL. Paste that URL here.

    If it still fails due to the network closing the connection

    Then we’ll create 2 smaller CORE gists (5 files each) using the same Pick-LatestScript function—this avoids the “many-files-in-one-upload” problem you hit earlier.

    Also: don’t run it inside gist_repo, and don’t include .zip—gh gist create cannot upload binary anyway (as you already saw).

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    )
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project_gist_share\gist_repo>
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project_gist_share\gist_repo> gh gist create $core -d "Oguta review: CORE scripts for evidence-pack build (text-only)"
    no matches found for .\scripts\litreview_pipeline_pc_v2_2_0.py
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project_gist_share\gist_repo>

    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    Good — the push succeeded, so the code snapshot is now stored in the secret gist git repo.

    But: why I still can’t read it yet (and how we fix it fast)

    Your gist now contains many files (and at least one binary: scripts_share.zip). My web fetch tool can open your earlier single-file gist fine, but it fails on this multi-file gist with a (400) OK fetch error. In practice, the fastest way to proceed is to create a second “core-text-only” secret gist containing just the handful of scripts I need to read first (no binaries, no huge batch). That will be accessible and we can continue immediately with your exact pipeline, not a rewrite.

    Step 1 (do this now): create a “CORE_SCRIPTS” secret gist (6–10 files only, text only)

    Run this from your project root (not _gist_share), so it uploads the original scripts directly:

    PowerShell
    $core = @(
  ".\scripts\litreview_pipeline_pc_v2_2_0.py",
  ".\scripts\build_litreview_pipeline_v2_0_1.py",

  ".\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py",
  ".\scripts\find_grobid_outputs_v1.py",
  ".\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v2.py",
  ".\scripts\grobid_retry_missing_tei_v1.py",

  ".\scripts\json_md_coverage_vs_included_v1.py",
  ".\scripts\md_section_coverage_included_v1.py",
  ".\scripts\auto_extraction_discrepancy_scan_v1.py",
  ".\scripts\build_extraction_audit_pack_v1.py"
)

gh gist create $core -d "Oguta review: CORE scripts for evidence-pack build (text-only)"

    This produces a new gist URL. Paste that URL here.
    (Keep your big gist as the archive; this small gist is the one I can reliably open and work from.)

    Step 2 (in parallel, on your machine): generate the TEI coverage report (so we don’t guess)

    This uses your existing assets and gives us the definitive “what’s missing” list before OCR/GROBID.

    2A) Identify your latest screening registry file

    PowerShell
    $reg = Get-ChildItem .\screening_v1 -Filter "study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING*.csv" |
  Sort-Object LastWriteTime -Descending |
  Select-Object -First 1

$reg.FullName

    2B) Run the coverage script help (so we know exact CLI args)

    PowerShell
    python .\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py --help

    Copy/paste the help text into chat (that single paste lets me give you the exact next commands without guessing your flags/paths).

    Step 3 (quick clean-up that will prevent future sharing/tooling issues)

    Remove the binary zip from the big gist repo (it’s not needed anymore and it can cause tooling/fetch problems):

    PowerShell
    cd .\_gist_share\gist_repo
git rm .\scripts_share.zip
git commit -m "Remove binary zip (keep text snapshots only)"
git push
cd ..\..

    What I will do immediately after you paste the CORE_SCRIPTS gist link

    1. Read your actual v2.2.0 pipeline entrypoint and confirm the “true run order” you’re using now.
    2. Lock in your actual folder conventions (PDF root, GROBID output root, registry locations) directly from your scripts (no assumptions).
    3. Produce a minimal set of additions (PowerShell-friendly) that plug into your pipeline:
    • OCR gate (only scanned PDFs)
    • TEI → section-aware chunker
    • evidence-backed extraction writer (value + snippet + TEI chunk id into registry)

    No re-architecture—just targeted upgrades.

    Paste the CORE_SCRIPTS gist URL (from Step 1) and the --help output (Step 2B) and we’ll move to the next concrete command you run.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project_gist_share\gist_repo> git push
    Enumerating objects: 119, done.
    Counting objects: 100% (119/119), done.
    Delta compression using up to 8 threads
    Compressing objects: 100% (118/118), done.
    Writing objects: 100% (118/118), 269.86 KiB | 2.96 MiB/s, done.
    Total 118 (delta 14), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 0 (from 0)
    remote: Resolving deltas: 100% (14/14), done.
    To https://gist.github.com/a090a07017c45f512d920799f538d30e.git
    b6ed9e7..f31b8a0 main -> main
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project_gist_share\gist_repo>

    So pushed successfully.

    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    GitHub gists (even when used as a git remote) do not support directories, so the push was rejected the moment it saw the scripts/ folder. That’s exactly what this line means:

    remote: Gist does not support directories... scripts

    So the fix is: push your scripts as top-level files (flattened names), and include a small FILEMAP that preserves the original paths. This is the most “you-specific” way forward because you already staged everything cleanly in .\_gist_share\scripts\ and you already have a working gist remote.

    Do this (PowerShell; copy/paste)

    1) Go to the gist repo and clean it back to the remote state

    PowerShell
    cd C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project\_gist_share\gist_repo

git fetch origin
git reset --hard origin/main
git clean -fdx

    2) Copy RUN_CONTEXT + TREE into the gist repo root (optional but useful)

    PowerShell
    Copy-Item ..\RUN_CONTEXT.md .\RUN_CONTEXT.md -Force

# optional (if you want the tree file in the gist too)
if (Test-Path ..\..\scripts\_TREE.txt) { Copy-Item ..\..\scripts\_TREE.txt .\_TREE.txt -Force }

    3) Flatten your staged scripts into gist-safe filenames (no folders)

    This copies every file from ..\scripts (your staged share folder) into the gist root with a safe name like: S__grobid_batch_process_cli_v2.py (and if any were in subfolders, those get encoded too).

    PowerShell
    $srcRoot = (Resolve-Path ..\scripts).Path
$map = New-Object System.Collections.Generic.List[object]

Get-ChildItem $srcRoot -Recurse -File | ForEach-Object {
    $rel = $_.FullName.Substring($srcRoot.Length).TrimStart('\')

    # turn any path separators into "__" so gist sees it as a single filename
    $safe = "S__" + ($rel -replace '[\\\/]', '__')

    Copy-Item $_.FullName (Join-Path (Get-Location) $safe) -Force

    $map.Add([pscustomobject]@{
        safe_name        = $safe
        original_relpath = $rel
    })
}

$map | Export-Csv -NoTypeInformation -Encoding utf8 .\FILEMAP.csv

    4) Confirm there are no directories in the gist repo now

    PowerShell
    Get-ChildItem -Directory

    This must return nothing.

    5) Commit + push (this time it will be accepted)

    PowerShell
    git add -A
git commit -m "Add scripts snapshot (flattened for gist; includes FILEMAP)"
git push

    Your gist URL stays the same: https://gist.github.com/Achionye/a090a07017c45f512d920799f538d30e

    Why this solves your exact blocker

    • You keep one private-ish share link (secret gist).
    • You bypass the gh gist create multi-file upload that your network keeps dropping.
    • You satisfy the hard gist limitation: no directories.
    • FILEMAP.csv preserves structure, so I can still refer to “scripts/xyz.py” logically while reading S__... files.

    Once the push succeeds, tell me “pushed successfully” and I’ll immediately start: pipeline entrypoint identification → TEI coverage workflow → minimal additions (OCR gate + TEI chunker + evidence-backed extraction writer) based on your actual code.

    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    Using a secret gist and then pushing your files to it via git will not make them publicly listed or discoverable on GitHub, but it also does not make them truly private: a secret gist is accessible to anyone who has the URL, and its contents can be viewed/downloaded without authentication if the link is shared or leaked; if you need access-controlled privacy, you’d need a private GitHub repo (or another storage option with permissions).

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    In one paragraph, confirm whether this approach will make my files public.

    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    That error is coming from the GitHub Gist HTTP upload path (what gh gist create uses). With 115 files in one request, GitHub (or something in-between your machine and GitHub—proxy/firewall) is terminating the connection:

    wsarecv: An existing connection was forcibly closed by the remote host

    So nothing is wrong with your staging. It’s the upload method.

    You have two fixes that work on Windows PowerShell with your exact setup. The best one (most reliable, least fiddly) is to treat the gist as a git remote and git push the files. That avoids the “multi-file API POST” that keeps failing.

    Fix (Recommended): Create a secret gist, then push your scripts to it via git

    This is custom-fit to your reality because:

    • you already have git set up
    • you already have a clean staged folder: .\_gist_share\scripts\
    • you already have a small snapshot zip (347 KB) if we want to include it too
    • pushing via git handles lots of files cleanly and allows binaries (zip) too

    Step 1 — Create an empty secret gist (1 small file only)

    This should succeed because it’s a tiny upload:

    PowerShell
    # Create a secret gist (default is secret)
$gistUrl = gh gist create .\_gist_share\RUN_CONTEXT.md -d "Oguta review project - scripts snapshot (git-pushed)"
$gistUrl

    You’ll see a URL like: https://gist.github.com/Achionye/<GIST_ID>

    Step 2 — Clone the gist as a git repo

    Extract the gist id and clone:

    PowerShell
    $gistId = ($gistUrl -split "/")[-1]
$gistId

git clone "https://gist.github.com/$gistId.git" .\_gist_share\gist_repo

    Step 3 — Copy your staged scripts into the gist repo (preserve folders)

    PowerShell
    # Copy the whole staged scripts folder into the gist repo
robocopy .\_gist_share\scripts .\_gist_share\gist_repo\scripts /S /NFL /NDL /NJH /NJS /NP

# Optional: also include your scripts tree file if you want
if (Test-Path .\scripts\_TREE.txt) { Copy-Item .\scripts\_TREE.txt .\_gist_share\gist_repo\_TREE.txt -Force }

# Optional: include the zip too (git push supports binary)
Copy-Item .\_gist_share\scripts_share.zip .\_gist_share\gist_repo\scripts_share.zip -Force

    Step 4 — Commit + push

    PowerShell
    cd .\_gist_share\gist_repo

git status
git add -A
git commit -m "Add scripts snapshot (staged, no outputs)"
git push

cd ..\..

    Step 5 — Paste the gist URL here

    Use the URL printed in Step 1 ($gistUrl).

    This method avoids the exact failure mode you hit and will work even if your network kills large “API style” uploads.

    Fix (Alternative): Split into several smaller gists (works, but messier)

    If for any reason git push is blocked but API uploads work for small batches, we can split by “script families” (you clearly have these in your tree: grobid/coverage/pipeline/enrich/audit). I can give you exact split commands, but the git-push gist above is the cleanest and most consistent.

    Why I’m insisting on the git-push gist (not generic—this is based on your output)

    • You already confirmed 115 files staged.
    • You already confirmed your zip is small, but gh gist create refuses binary (binary file not supported).
    • You already confirmed gh gist create works for single text files (you successfully created _TREE.txt earlier). So the only path that satisfies all constraints is: create gist → clone as repo → git add/commit/push.

    Run the “Recommended” steps above. Once you paste the gist link, I’ll immediately:

    1. identify your actual pipeline entrypoints and current “truth” versions (you have many v* scripts),
    2. map your existing TEI coverage + GROBID retry logic,
    3. propose the minimal additions for OCR gate + chunk/index + evidence-backed extraction as patches to your existing scripts, not a rewrite.

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> Get-ChildItem ._gist_share\scripts -Recurse -File | Measure-Object

    Count : 115

    Sum :

    Minimum :

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> $files = Get-ChildItem ._gist_share\scripts -Recurse -File |

    Where-Object { _.Extension -match '^\.(py|ps1|md|txt|json|ya?ml|toml|ini|cfg)' }
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> # create the secret gist (default is secret: do NOT add --public)
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> gh gist create $files.FullName ._gist_share\RUN_CONTEXT.md `
    -d "Oguta review project - scripts snapshot (no outputs)"

    • Creating gist with multiple files
      X Failed to create gist: read tcp 10.144.107.121:56449->140.82.121.5:443: wsarecv: An existing connection was forcibly closed by the remote host.
      PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>
    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    Two separate things happened, both explained by how gh gist create works on Windows:

    1) --secret is not a valid flag (because “secret” is the default)

    Your gh output literally says:

    • -p, --public List the gist publicly (default "secret")

    So:

    • Secret gist = default → don’t pass anything
    • Public gist → add --public

    That part is normal.

    2) gh gist create (CLI) does not upload binary files like .zip

    That’s why you got:

    binary file not supported

    So the “zip the folder then gist it” approach won’t work via gh CLI.

    What will work (custom to your setup)

    You already staged the right stuff into .\_gist_share\scripts\ (and the size is small). The correct move is: gist the text files themselves (py/ps1/md/json/yaml…), not a zip.

    Do this now (PowerShell commands that will work in your project)

    Step A — sanity check: how many files are in your staged share?

    PowerShell
    Get-ChildItem .\_gist_share\scripts -Recurse -File | Measure-Object

    Step B — create ONE secret gist from all staged script files + RUN_CONTEXT

    This uses your staged folder (already excludes out_litreview_pc_v1_2 and __pycache__), so it’s exactly aligned with your repo.

    PowerShell
    # collect staged files (text-based only)
$files = Get-ChildItem .\_gist_share\scripts -Recurse -File |
  Where-Object { $_.Extension -match '^\.(py|ps1|md|txt|json|ya?ml|toml|ini|cfg)$' }

# create the secret gist (default is secret: do NOT add --public)
gh gist create $files.FullName .\_gist_share\RUN_CONTEXT.md `
  -d "Oguta review project - scripts snapshot (no outputs)"

    That should output a gist URL. Paste it here.

    If you hit another “stream error / CANCEL” (split into 3 gists, matching your actual script themes)

    Your scripts/_TREE.txt shows you have clear “families” of scripts. Splitting reduces upload complexity and makes it easier for me to review.

    Gist 1 — pipeline entrypoints + core orchestration

    PowerShell
    $files1 = Get-ChildItem .\_gist_share\scripts -Recurse -File |
  Where-Object { $_.Name -match 'litreview_pipeline|build_litreview_pipeline|pipeline|manifest' }

gh gist create $files1.FullName .\_gist_share\RUN_CONTEXT.md `
  -d "Oguta review - core pipeline entrypoints"

    Gist 2 — GROBID + coverage diagnostics (you clearly have these)

    PowerShell
    $files2 = Get-ChildItem .\_gist_share\scripts -Recurse -File |
  Where-Object { $_.Name -match 'grobid|tei_coverage|json_md_coverage|md_section_coverage|find_grobid' }

gh gist create $files2.FullName `
  -d "Oguta review - GROBID + TEI/MD coverage tooling"

    Gist 3 — metadata enrichment + OpenAlex/Crossref + audits

    PowerShell
    $files3 = Get-ChildItem .\_gist_share\scripts -Recurse -File |
  Where-Object { $_.Name -match 'openalex|crossref|metadata_enrich|audit|discrepancy|dedupe' }

gh gist create $files3.FullName `
  -d "Oguta review - metadata enrichment + audit tooling"

    Why this is the right “custom” next step for your project

    Because (from your tree) you already have:

    • batch GROBID extraction + retries
    • TEI coverage vs included logic
    • discrepancy scans / audit pack scripts

    So once I can actually read the scripts (not just names), I will not propose a new architecture. I’ll produce a delta patch plan that adds only what you’re missing next:

    1. OCR gate (only when PDF is scanned) that plugs into your existing “retry missing TEI” flow
    2. TEI → section-aware chunker (Methods/Validation/Preprocessing)
    3. evidence-backed extraction writer (value + snippet + TEI chunk id → merged into your registry)

    All as PowerShell-friendly entrypoints.

    Run Step B first. If it fails, run the 3-gist split. Then paste the gist link(s) here and we proceed immediately to Step 1: TEI coverage inventory (using your existing tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py logic).

    ._gist_share\scripts_share.zip .\_gist_share\RUN_CONTEXT.md
    --secret `

    gh gist create .\_gist_share\scripts_share.zip
    ._gist_share\RUN_CONTEXT.md `
