    • Token: gho_************************************
    • Token scopes: 'gist', 'read:org', 'repo', 'workflow'
      failed to collect files for posting: failed to upload ._gist_share\scripts_share.zip: binary file not supported
      PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>"

    What's up?

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    _TREE.txt

    https://gist.github.com/Achionye/20a9749d0f9ba83adce90ed5b7fd66f9

    You’re right to call that out. I can tailor this to your actual project because you already showed me your real scripts/ contents in _TREE.txt. 1

    What you need now is a repeatable, low-failure way to share your scripts privately (as privately as GitHub allows) so I can (a) understand your current pipeline entrypoints and (b) modify/add only the missing pieces (OCR gate → chunk/index → evidence-backed extraction) without breaking what already works.

    Below is a custom plan that matches your folder (including the exact directories you have that must be excluded, like scripts\out_litreview_pc_v1_2\ and scripts\__pycache__). 1

    First: one important reality about “Secret gists”

    A Secret gist is not public/listed, but it is not access-controlled: anyone with the link can view it. That’s usually fine for scripts, but don’t include tokens/keys.

    Step 1 — Create a “share pack” from your scripts (excluding your output dirs)

    From your repo root (C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project):

    1A) Make a staging folder

    PowerShell
    Remove-Item -Recurse -Force .\_gist_share -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path .\_gist_share | Out-Null

    1B) Copy only what matters from scripts\ (custom excludes for your repo)

    You have these inside scripts\: out_litreview_pc_v1_2\caches\, out_litreview_pc_v1_2\sections\, and __pycache__ (per your _TREE.txt). We exclude them. 1

    PowerShell
    robocopy .\scripts .\_gist_share\scripts `
  *.py *.ps1 *.md *.txt *.json *.yml *.yaml *.toml *.ini *.cfg `
  /S `
  /XD __pycache__ out_litreview_pc_v1_2 `
  /NFL /NDL /NJH /NJS /NP

    1C) Add a tiny run-context file (so I can reason about your pipeline versions)

    PowerShell
    @"
RUN CONTEXT (for script review)

- Python: 3.14.3
- PowerShell: 5.1
- Project root: oguta_review_project
- Key scripts I currently run (if known):
  - litreview_pipeline_pc_v2_2_0.py (or whichever)
  - build_litreview_pipeline_v2_0_1.py (if used)
- Where PDFs live (folder name only, no private paths if you prefer):
  - forlitrev_pdfs\  (yes/no)
- Where GROBID outputs are written (folder name only):
  - (e.g., grobid_out\ or tei\ etc.)
- How GROBID is run:
  - docker/local + URL/port if applicable
"@ | Out-File -Encoding utf8 .\_gist_share\RUN_CONTEXT.md

    1D) Zip it (this avoids gist “multi-file” flakiness and preserves structure)

    PowerShell
    Remove-Item .\_gist_share\scripts_share.zip -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Compress-Archive -Path .\_gist_share\scripts\* -DestinationPath .\_gist_share\scripts_share.zip -Force

(Get-Item .\_gist_share\scripts_share.zip).Length

    Step 2 — Create the secret gist (the reliable way)

    This uploads one zip + your context, which is much less likely to hit the “stream CANCEL” issue you saw when trying to gist a big tree file. 1

    PowerShell
    gh auth status

gh gist create `
  .\_gist_share\scripts_share.zip `
  .\_gist_share\RUN_CONTEXT.md `
  --secret `
  -d "Oguta review project - scripts snapshot (no outputs)"

    Paste the resulting gist URL here.

    If you still get “stream error: CANCEL” (custom fallback that fits your script folder)

    If your connection is flaky or the zip is still large, do this split upload (two smaller zips):

    PowerShell
    # Zip only *.py (usually the bulk, but still smaller than everything)
Remove-Item .\_gist_share\scripts_py.zip -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Compress-Archive -Path (Get-ChildItem .\_gist_share\scripts -Recurse -File -Filter *.py).FullName `
  -DestinationPath .\_gist_share\scripts_py.zip -Force

# Zip everything else (md/txt/json/yaml/ps1)
Remove-Item .\_gist_share\scripts_misc.zip -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Compress-Archive -Path (Get-ChildItem .\_gist_share\scripts -Recurse -File |
  Where-Object { $_.Extension -ne ".py" }).FullName `
  -DestinationPath .\_gist_share\scripts_misc.zip -Force

gh gist create .\_gist_share\scripts_py.zip .\_gist_share\scripts_misc.zip .\_gist_share\RUN_CONTEXT.md `
  --secret -d "Oguta review project - scripts snapshot (split)"

    What I will do immediately once you share the gist (specific to your scripts)

    Because I can already see your actual script names, I will start by mapping and “locking” your current pipeline around these (already in your tree): 1

    • Pipeline entrypoints: litreview_pipeline_pc_v2_2_0.py, build_litreview_pipeline_v2_0_1.py
    • GROBID + recovery: grobid_batch_process_cli_v2.py, grobid_batch_process_cli_v3_retry.py, grobid_retry_missing_tei_v1.py
    • Coverage/diagnostics: find_grobid_outputs_v1.py, find_grobid_json_md_outputs_v1.py, tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py, json_md_coverage_vs_included_v1.py, md_section_coverage_included_v1.py
    • Audit tooling: auto_extraction_discrepancy_scan_v1.py, build_extraction_audit_pack_v1.py
    • Metadata enrichment lineage: metadata_enrich_v4_1.py, openalex_enrich_and_refine_v2.py, etc.

    Then I’ll produce a delta plan (not a rewrite) that adds exactly 3 new capabilities you don’t yet have as explicit modules:

    1. OCR gate (only for scanned PDFs)
    2. TEI → section-aware chunker (Methods/Validation/Preprocessing chunks)
    3. Evidence-backed extractor (writes value + evidence_chunk_id + snippet back into your registry)

    All PowerShell-runnable.

    Run Step 1–2 above and paste the gist link. After that, we begin with TEI coverage vs included using your existing tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py logic (and I’ll adjust it only if your folder naming needs a small fix).

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    Your response is not helpful as it is generic. The essence of giving you context and my peculiar realities is for a custom output that applies exactly to my peculiarities. Revise this your response so this project can proceed.

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    _TREE.txt

    https://gist.github.com/Achionye/20a9749d0f9ba83adce90ed5b7fd66f9

    Do it with Secret Gists, but don’t try to upload your whole project tree. The reliable way is:

    1. stage only the code/config you want to share (exclude outputs/caches),
    2. zip that staged folder (keeps directory structure),
    3. upload the zip as a secret gist.

    This avoids (a) gist size/stream errors, and (b) the fact that gists don’t preserve folders nicely unless you upload an archive. 1

    Step-by-step (PowerShell 5.1) — “scripts snapshot” secret gist

    1) Create a clean staging folder

    From repo root:

    PowerShell
    # clean staging area
Remove-Item -Recurse -Force .\_gist_share\scripts_share -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path .\_gist_share\scripts_share | Out-Null

    2) Copy only the files you actually want to share

    This copies code + small config/docs, and excludes typical junk folders (__pycache__, outputs, caches).

    PowerShell
    robocopy .\scripts .\_gist_share\scripts_share `
  *.py *.ps1 *.md *.txt *.json *.yml *.yaml *.toml *.ini *.cfg `
  /S `
  /XD __pycache__ out_litreview_pc_v1_2 caches sections `
  /NFL /NDL /NJH /NJS /NP

    If you have other “output” directories inside scripts\, add them to /XD.

    3) Add a minimal run-context file (so I can run/understand your pipeline)

    Create RUN_CONTEXT.md at repo root (or in _gist_share):

    PowerShell
    @"
# Run context (shared snapshot)

- OS: Windows (PowerShell 5.1)
- Python: 3.14.3
- Repo root: oguta_review_project
- How I run the pipeline (high level):
  1) ...
  2) ...
- Key folders (local paths OK to redact):
  - PDFs: .\forlitrev_pdfs\
  - GROBID outputs: ...
  - Registries: ...
- GROBID runtime:
  - Docker/local:
  - Endpoint/command used:
"@ | Out-File -Encoding utf8 .\_gist_share\RUN_CONTEXT.md

    4) (Strongly recommended) export your Python deps

    If you don’t already have requirements.txt / pyproject.toml, do:

    PowerShell
    python -m pip freeze | Out-File -Encoding utf8 .\_gist_share\requirements_freeze.txt

    5) Zip the staged snapshot (keeps folders)

    PowerShell
    Remove-Item .\_gist_share\scripts_share.zip -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Compress-Archive -Path .\_gist_share\scripts_share\* -DestinationPath .\_gist_share\scripts_share.zip

    Check size (important):

    PowerShell
    (Get-Item .\_gist_share\scripts_share.zip).Length

    If it’s huge (tens of MB), we’ll split it (I’ll give you the split commands), but usually scripts-only is fine.

    6) Create the secret gist (upload zip + context + deps)

    PowerShell
    gh auth status

gh gist create `
  .\_gist_share\scripts_share.zip `
  .\_gist_share\RUN_CONTEXT.md `
  .\_gist_share\requirements_freeze.txt `
  --secret `
  -d "Oguta review project: scripts snapshot (staged+zipped)"

    That prints a gist URL—paste it here.

    If gh gist create still throws a stream/CANCEL error

    That’s almost always upload size/connection. Two reliable fallbacks:

    Fallback A: Upload only the zip first

    PowerShell
    gh gist create .\_gist_share\scripts_share.zip --secret -d "scripts snapshot zip only"

    Fallback B: Split into 2 smaller zips (no extra tools)

    Example split by file type:

    PowerShell
    Remove-Item .\_gist_share\scripts_py.zip -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Compress-Archive -Path (Get-ChildItem .\_gist_share\scripts_share -Recurse -File -Filter *.py).FullName `
  -DestinationPath .\_gist_share\scripts_py.zip

Remove-Item .\_gist_share\scripts_misc.zip -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Compress-Archive -Path (Get-ChildItem .\_gist_share\scripts_share -Recurse -File | Where-Object {$_.Extension -ne ".py"}).FullName `
  -DestinationPath .\_gist_share\scripts_misc.zip

gh gist create .\_gist_share\scripts_py.zip .\_gist_share\scripts_misc.zip --secret -d "scripts snapshot split"

    After you paste the gist link: our next “step 1” in the pipeline

    I’ll read your current scripts and produce a zero-rewrite plan:

    1. identify your “current truth” pipeline entrypoint(s) (which litreview_pipeline_* you’re actually on now),
    2. generate an Evidence Pack Builder v1 that reuses your existing coverage scripts (tei_coverage_vs_included_*, find_grobid_outputs_*, grobid_retry_missing_tei_*), and
    3. add only the missing layer: OCR gate + TEI→chunks + index + evidence-backed extraction outputs.

    Paste the secret gist link when it’s created.

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    Why I do not want to use a repo is so that it doesn't get public. So gist is preferred here. Direct me

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    _TREE.txt

    https://gist.github.com/Achionye/20a9749d0f9ba83adce90ed5b7fd66f9

    I can see your scripts/ inventory now from the _TREE.txt gist, including (importantly) your existing GROBID and coverage tooling like grobid_batch_process_cli_v2.py, grobid_retry_missing_tei_v1.py, find_grobid_outputs_v1.py, tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py, plus your pipeline drivers (litreview_pipeline_pc_v2_2_0.py, build_litreview_pipeline_v2_0_1.py, etc.). 1

    That’s great news: we’re not building from scratch—we’re going to extend what you already have into an “evidence pack builder” (inventory → OCR gate → TEI gap-fill → chunk/index → evidence-backed extraction).

    Below is the step-by-step next move.

    Step 1 (now): share your actual scripts safely (not just the tree)

    Why your gh gist create oguta_review_project_folder.txt failed

    That stream error: CANCEL; received from peer is usually one of:

    • the file is too large for gist / upload got interrupted
    • transient network issue
    • the file path/name didn’t resolve as expected (your tree ... command looked like it may have been mistyped in the pasted console line)

    Fix: generate a small repo tree file correctly (optional)

    If you still want a full-tree snapshot, do it like this:

    PowerShell
    # ASCII tree (avoids box-drawing chars)
cmd /c "tree /a /f" | Out-File -Encoding utf8 .\oguta_review_project_tree.txt

# check file size
(Get-Item .\oguta_review_project_tree.txt).Length

    If it’s huge (multi‑MB), don’t gist it—zip it or skip it (we already have the scripts tree).

    Step 2: create a gist of your scripts in a way that won’t choke

    Option A (best for many files): make a GitHub repo and push

    Because you have lots of scripts (100+), a normal repo is honestly the most robust. It preserves folders and lets me browse everything cleanly.

    PowerShell
    # from your project root
git status
git remote -v

# if no origin yet, add one (replace URL with your repo)
git remote add origin https://github.com/<your-username>/<your-repo>.git

git push -u origin main

    Then you paste the repo link here.

    Option B (still fine): split into 2–4 gists by category

    If you prefer gists, don’t try to upload everything in one shot. Create separate gists:

    Gist 1: “pipeline drivers + configs” Include:

    • litreview_pipeline_pc_v2_2_0.py 1
    • build_litreview_pipeline_v2_0_1.py 1
    • any requirements file (requirements.txt / pyproject.toml / environment.yml) if you have it

    Gist 2: “GROBID + coverage” Include:

    • grobid_batch_process_cli_v2.py 1
    • grobid_retry_missing_tei_v1.py 1
    • find_grobid_outputs_v1.py 1
    • tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py 1
    • json_md_coverage_vs_included_v1.py 1

    Gist 3: “download + metadata enrich” Include:

    • latest download_from_manifest_cli_v*.py 1
    • latest metadata_enrich_v*.py 1
    • OpenAlex scripts you’re currently using (e.g., openalex_enrich_and_refine_v2.py) 1

    PowerShell to create a gist from selected files:

    PowerShell
    gh auth status
gh --version

$files = @(
  ".\scripts\litreview_pipeline_pc_v2_2_0.py",
  ".\scripts\build_litreview_pipeline_v2_0_1.py",
  ".\scripts\grobid_batch_process_cli_v2.py",
  ".\scripts\grobid_retry_missing_tei_v1.py",
  ".\scripts\find_grobid_outputs_v1.py",
  ".\scripts\tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py",
  ".\scripts\json_md_coverage_vs_included_v1.py"
)

gh gist create $files --secret -d "oguta review scripts - core pipeline + grobid coverage"

    If it still errors, zip first:

    PowerShell
    Compress-Archive -Path .\scripts\*.py -DestinationPath .\scripts_snapshot.zip
gh gist create .\scripts_snapshot.zip --secret -d "oguta review scripts snapshot (zipped)"

    Step 3 (immediately after you share code): we start with TEI coverage + evidence-pack inventory

    Once I can see the scripts, the first concrete build step will be:

    1. Run (or refine) your existing TEI coverage script (tei_coverage_vs_included_v2.py) to produce a definitive table: included papers vs TEI present/missing. 1
    2. From that, generate the “gap-fill manifest” for:
      • OCR-needed PDFs
      • GROBID-missing TEIs
    3. Only then do we add the next module: chunking + index + evidence snippet extraction.

    That sequence prevents reprocessing and keeps everything auditable.

    One important environment note (prevents future “limited by packages” pain)

    You’re on Python 3.14.3. That may be totally fine, but many PDF/ML libraries sometimes lag on brand-new Python versions (wheels not built yet). If you hit install errors later (FAISS, some PDF libs, etc.), the “no limitations” fix is to keep a second environment on Python 3.12 just for the pipeline.

    PowerShell
    py -3.12 -m venv .venv312
.\.venv312\Scripts\Activate.ps1
python -m pip install -U pip

    No need to do this now unless installs start failing—but it’s the standard escape hatch.

    Paste either (A) your GitHub repo link or (B) the core-script gist(s), and I’ll start by mapping your pipeline “as-is” and proposing the minimal edits to turn it into the evidence-pack builder (PowerShell entrypoints included).

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> python --version
    Python 3.14.3
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> $PSVersionTable.PSVersion

    Major Minor Build Revision

    5 1 26100 1591

    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> tree .\scripts /f > .\scripts_TREE.txt
    PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> tree C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project /f .oguta_review_project_folder.txtPS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> gh gist create oguta_review_project_folder.txt

    • Creating gist oguta_review_project_folder.txt
      X Failed to create gist: stream error: stream ID 1; CANCEL; received from peer
      PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project> gh gist create .\scripts_TREE.txt
    • Creating gist _TREE.txt
      ✓ Created secret gist _TREE.txt
      https://gist.github.com/Achionye/20a9749d0f9ba83adce90ed5b7fd66f9
      PS C:\Users\VISITOR\Documents\journal\oguta_review_project>
    gpt-5.2-search
    No Sources

    Perfect. If you gist your scripts/ folder (plus a tiny amount of “run context”), I can work from your existing pipeline and we’ll only add/modify what’s necessary.

    Below is the step-by-step way to do this safely (no secrets, no PDFs), using PowerShell, with two options (GitHub web UI or gh CLI). Then we’ll start Step 1 of the build (Inventory → OCR gate → GROBID gap-fill → chunk/index).

    Step 0 — Create a safe “share snapshot” branch (recommended)

    This protects your main work and avoids accidentally leaking paths/tokens.

    PowerShell
    git checkout -b share-scripts-snapshot
git status

    Step 1 — Sanitize what will be shared (critical)

    Do not gist:

    • PDFs (copyright + huge)
    • Any API keys/tokens (OpenAI keys, SerpAPI, etc.)
    • Any files that contain private paths or machine usernames if you care about privacy

    Quick scan for common secret patterns (PowerShell)

    From repo root:

    PowerShell
    # Quick grep-like scan for likely secrets
Get-ChildItem -Recurse -File |
  Where-Object { $_.Extension -match '\.(py|ps1|json|yml|yaml|toml|env|txt|md)$' } |
  ForEach-Object {
    $hits = Select-String -Path $_.FullName -Pattern "api[_-]?key|token|secret|bearer|OPENAI|SERPAPI|password" -SimpleMatch -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
    if ($hits) { $_.FullName }
  } |
  Sort-Object -Unique

    If you find secrets:

    • replace with placeholders like YOUR_API_KEY_HERE
    • or move secrets into a non-shared file like .env (and don’t gist it)

    Step 2 — Prepare a minimal “run context” file (so I don’t have to guess)

    Inside scripts/ (or repo root), add a small markdown file:

    RUN_CONTEXT.md (recommended contents)

    • OS (Windows 10/11), PowerShell version
    • python --version
    • how you currently run the pipeline (the intended order of scripts)
    • where inputs live (example paths; can be placeholders)
    • where outputs go
    • whether you already have GROBID running (Docker or local) and how you call it

    This one file saves a lot of back-and-forth.

    You can generate versions quickly:

    PowerShell
    python --version
$PSVersionTable.PSVersion

    Step 3 — Generate a “tree” of your scripts folder (helps orientation)

    From repo root:

    PowerShell
    tree .\scripts /f > .\scripts\_TREE.txt

    Also helpful: include your dependencies file if present (requirements.txt, environment.yml, pyproject.toml, etc.).

    Step 4 — Create the Gist (two ways)

    Option A (fastest): GitHub CLI (gh) from PowerShell

    If you have GitHub CLI installed and authenticated:

    PowerShell
    gh auth status

    Then create a gist containing only code/config/docs (no outputs):

    PowerShell
    $files = Get-ChildItem .\scripts -Recurse -File -Include `
  *.py,*.ps1,*.ipynb,*.md,*.txt,*.json,*.yml,*.yaml,*.toml,requirements.txt,pyproject.toml,poetry.lock

# Create a public gist (or change to --secret)
gh gist create $files.FullName --public -d "Oguta RS-ML scoping review pipeline scripts snapshot"

    If it errors due to size/too many files:

    • make 2 gists:
      1. scripts/*.py + scripts/*.ps1
      2. RUN_CONTEXT.md, config files, small docs

    Option B: Web UI (manual, but reliable)

    • GitHub → “New gist”
    • Drag-drop:
      • scripts/ code files (not PDFs, not large outputs)
      • _TREE.txt
      • RUN_CONTEXT.md
    • Create as Secret gist if you prefer

    Step 5 — Paste the Gist link here

    Once you paste it, I’ll do this immediately (in order, no reinvention):

    1. Read your current pipeline and infer the “true run graph” (what depends on what).
    2. Identify what you already do for:
      • PDF integrity checks
      • GROBID TEI extraction
      • metadata enrichment (Crossref/OpenAlex)
      • dedupe / ID strategy
    3. Propose a minimal set of new scripts (or modifications) to implement:
      • OCR gate (OCRmyPDF only when needed)
      • TEI gap-fill (only missing TEI)
      • TEI→section-aware chunking
      • index build (Chroma or FAISS)
      • evidence-backed field extraction JSONL → registry merge

    And I’ll keep everything PowerShell-friendly (entrypoints you can run like .\run_step3_chunk.ps1 etc.).

    One non-negotiable to make this “not limited”

    Along with your scripts, please include any one of these if you have it:

    • requirements.txt or environment.yml or pyproject.toml

    Without pinned deps, reproducibility becomes the main bottleneck later.

    When the gist is up, drop the link and we’ll start with Step 1: Inventory + TEI coverage report (so you can see exactly what’s missing before we touch OCR/GROBID).

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    Good. Let's go about it step by step. As you see in the examples, I use Powershell. To save you some time, I think it's best to gist the files in script folder so that you will know my scripts and not have to start everything from scratch. That way you also know what to modify/add/change/refine. I already have my git set up.

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    CONTINUATION report.txt

    https://gist.github.com/Achionye/14f8232e3c597c997be21fd52ee2c71f

    2

    ocrmypdf

    https://pypi.org/project/ocrmypdf/

    3

    Grobid in batch mode - GROBID Documentation

    https://grobid.readthedocs.io/en/latest/Grobid-batch/

    4

    Chroma

    https://www.trychroma.com/

    5

    Faiss: A library for efficient similarity search

    https://engineering.fb.com/2017/03/29/data-infrastructure/faiss-a-library-for-efficient-similarity-search/

    Proceed like you’re building a closed, auditable “evidence pack” from your PDFs (full text + structure + traceable snippets), then run field extraction on top of that.

    You already have most of the hard pieces: a screening registry with include_final, DOI coverage, Crossref/OpenAlex enrichment, a PDF folder (forlitrev_pdfs), and ~215 PDFs already processed with GROBID TEI (plus you’ve already been thinking in terms of FAISS/Chroma indexing and manual audit sampling). 1

    Below is the “how we proceed” plan and the tools to make it essentially not limited.

    A. How we proceed (practical pipeline)

    Phase 0 — Freeze + inventory (so outputs stop drifting)

    1. Freeze a snapshot of:
    • screening_v1/study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING*.csv
    • your current scripts folder
    • forlitrev_pdfs/
    • existing GROBID outputs (TEI) 1
    1. Inventory what you already have (this matters because you should not re-run GROBID blindly if you already have good TEI for most papers). 1
      Deliverable: a table like:
    paper_iddoipdf_pathtei_pathocr_appliednotes

    Phase 1 — PDF “health” + OCR normalization (this removes 80% of extraction pain)

    You already wrote/ran a PDF magic-byte check idea—keep that as a gate. 1

    Then add OCR preprocessing only where needed:

    • If a PDF is scanned/image-only, GROBID and text chunking will be weak until you OCR it.
    • Use OCRmyPDF to add a selectable text layer while preserving the original page appearance. 2

    Deliverables:

    • forlitrev_pdfs/_quarantine_bad_magic/ (already in your plan) 1
    • forlitrev_pdfs_ocr/ (normalized OCR’d copies for the subset that need it)

    Phase 2 — Structured fulltext extraction (GROBID as the backbone)

    Use GROBID to produce TEI for any PDFs that don’t already have TEI. Your own report already identified GROBID as the decisive tool for extracting the methodological details that metadata APIs don’t have. 1

    GROBID supports batch processFullText extraction to TEI. 3

    Deliverables:

    • evidence_pack/fulltext_tei/*.tei.xml (one per paper)
    • (optional) evidence_pack/fulltext_md/*.md (converted from TEI for easier chunking)

    Phase 3 — Chunking + indexing (so you can “retrieve then extract”)

    Convert TEI/MD into section-aware chunks:

    • chunk metadata should include: {paper_id, doi, year_verified, section, subsection, chunk_id, source_path, char_start/char_end (or line numbers)}

    Then build a local index:

    • Chroma (good when you want metadata filtering + full-text-ish workflows) 4
    • or FAISS (excellent pure similarity search library; very fast) 5

    Deliverables:

    • evidence_pack/chunks.jsonl
    • evidence_pack/index/ (Chroma or FAISS)
    • a tiny “retrieval CLI” so you can do: “show me chunks about validation design / atmospheric correction / matchups”

    Phase 4 — Evidence-backed extraction to your registry (LLM-assisted, but auditable)

    Now you run extraction on retrieved chunks, not on “the whole internet” and not on model memory.

    Output should be machine-auditable:

    • extracted field value
    • evidence snippet (short)
    • chunk_id(s)
    • a confidence flag
    • “NR” if not explicitly found

    Deliverables:

    • evidence_pack/extracted_fields.jsonl
    • updated study_registry_SCREENING_WORKING_ENRICHED.csv with new columns like:
      • validation_type, split_design_notes, ac_method, matchup_window, uncertainty_reported, code_available, etc.
      • plus *_evidence_chunk_ids

    Phase 5 — Manual audit loop (small, stratified, continuous)

    You already did the right thing earlier: discrepancy scan + stratified manual audit sample. Keep that pattern, but now you’ll audit fields that drive the “critical” claims (validation leakage risk, preprocessing transparency, transferability tests). 1

    B. Tools you “already” have (based on your report)

    From your gist, your current toolset already includes:

    • Windows PowerShell + Python scripts driving the pipeline 1
    • Crossref REST for DOI metadata and year verification logic 1
    • OpenAlex for abstract access (reconstructed from inverted index) 1
    • GROBID TEI extraction already run for ~215 PDFs 1
    • You’ve already framed FAISS/Chroma as your local retrieval layer options 1

    So we’re not starting from scratch—you’re basically at “build evidence pack properly + finish ingestion coverage.”

    C. What to add so the work is “not limited”

    Think in three tiers: required, strongly recommended, optional but removes edge-case limits.

    C1) Required additions (for robust PDF extraction)

    1. OCRmyPDF + Tesseract + Ghostscript
    • Solves scanned PDFs / image-only papers (a common blocker).
    • OCRmyPDF is designed specifically to add an OCR text layer to PDFs. 2
    1. A reproducible GROBID runtime
    • Either Dockerized GROBID or a pinned local install.
    • Batch processFullText is the workhorse for TEI extraction. 3

    C2) Strongly recommended (to keep things scalable + auditable)

    1. A local “evidence pack” store in DuckDB or SQLite
    • Your CSV registries are great, but once you start storing chunks + evidence spans, a small DB makes querying/auditing far easier (e.g., “show all papers with validation_type=random AND N_matchups<50 AND tropical=1”).
    1. Vector store choice + a lexical fallback
    • Keep Chroma or FAISS for semantic retrieval. 4
    • Add a simple lexical search fallback (even just SQLite FTS or ripgrep over Markdown) for exact phrases like “atmospheric correction”, “ACOLITE”, “Sen2Cor”, “adjacency effect”, etc. (semantic search alone sometimes misses exact-method strings).
    1. A “paper_id ↔ pdf ↔ tei” naming convention
    • The #1 operational failure in evidence-pack projects is broken mapping. Make it deterministic:
      • file name = sanitized DOI when available
      • else = your internal paper_id

    C3) Optional (these remove remaining “limitations”)

    1. Table extraction tools (only if you truly need numeric tables)
    • If you want to pull matchup counts/metrics from tables reliably:
      • camelot (good for lattice-style tables)
      • tabula-py (often good, but depends on Java)
    • Many reviews don’t need this, because the key items (validation type, AC method, matchup window) are typically in Methods text, not only tables.
    1. A reference manager bridge
    • If your manuscript will be in Word/LaTeX, consider managing BibTeX/CSL properly:
      • Zotero (+ Better BibTeX) or JabRef
    • This doesn’t extract from PDFs per se, but it prevents citation chaos later.
    1. Workflow orchestration (Snakemake or Make)
    • Not essential, but it turns your pipeline into one command:
      • snakemake -j 8 build_evidence_pack
    • Big benefit: you can re-run only what changed (e.g., “only OCR new PDFs”, “only GROBID missing TEI”).

    D. The “no limitations” target state (what you’re aiming for)

    If you implement the above, the only remaining limitations will be non-technical (e.g., paywalled PDFs you don’t possess, or papers with extremely poor scan quality).

    Your end state should look like:

    • Registry layer (your current strength): included set + coded synthesis fields
    • Evidence layer (new): TEI/MD chunks + retrieval index
    • Extraction layer (new): LLM-assisted field extraction with evidence chunk IDs
    • Audit layer (you already started): discrepancy scans + stratified manual checks 1

    That architecture is what makes the project “not limited”: you can always (1) retrieve the exact source text, (2) re-extract fields, (3) re-audit, (4) regenerate tables/figures—without re-reading 268 papers end-to-end.

    If you want to proceed immediately without redesigning your repo: the next concrete build step is an “Evidence Pack Builder v1” script exactly like the one described in your own report (inventory TEI, fill missing with GROBID, chunk, index). 1

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    Very good. How do we proceed in getting my resources extracted and gathered from my pdfs? What tools would this require? You must have an idea of my current toolset. What additional do I need to make sure that this work is in no way limited?

    gpt-5.2-search

    1

    Remote sensing of inland waters: Challenges, progress and future directions

    https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0034425714003666

    2

    Ocean Color | NASA Earthdata

    https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/topics/ocean/ocean-color

    3

    A non-optically active lake salinity dataset by satellite remote sensing - Scientific Data

    https://www.nature.com/articles/s41597-025-05686-2

    4

    IOCCG Reports - IOCCG

    https://ioccg.org/what-we-do/ioccg-publications/ioccg-reports/

    5

    Spatial cross-validation and bootstrap for the assessment of prediction rules in remote sensing: The R package sperrorest

    https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/6352393

    6

    Sentinel-2 ACOLITE-DSF Aquatic Reflectance for the Conterminous United States

    https://www.usgs.gov/data/sentinel-2-acolite-dsf-aquatic-reflectance-conterminous-united-states

    7

    Seamless retrievals of chlorophyll-a from Sentinel-2 (MSI) and Sentinel-3 (OLCI) in inland and coastal waters: A machine-learning approach

    https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0034425719306248

    8

    Scoping studies: towards a methodological framework - White Rose Research Online

    https://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/00001618/

    9

    Scoping — PRISMA statement

    https://www.prisma-statement.org/scoping

    10

    Researchgate

    https://www.researchgate.net/publication/311523792_Cross-validation_strategies_for_data_with_temporal_spatial_hierarchical_or_phylogenetic_structure

    11

    Oguta Lake | Ramsar Sites Information Service

    https://rsis.ramsar.org/ris/1757

    12

    Lake Oguta | Oguta Lake | World Lake Database - ILEC

    https://wldb.ilec.or.jp/Display/html/3599

    13

    Assessment of the physico-chemical properties of Oguta Lake compared to the established values of the Federal Ministry of Environment, Nigeria: Water quality of Lake Oguta, Nigeria | Advances in Oceanography and Limnology

    https://www.pagepressjournals.org/index.php/aiol/article/view/aiol.2019.8522

    14

    Mdpi

    https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/14/8/1829

    15

    Mdpi

    https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/9/4/322

    16

    Optical characteristics of two contrasting Case 2 waters and their influence on remote sensing algorithms

    https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0278434302002224

    17

    Landsat 7

    https://www.usgs.gov/landsat-missions/landsat-7

    18

    Sentinel-2A | OPS Portal

    https://esoc.esa.int/content/sentinel-2a

    Summary (what to tighten + what to add)

    Your reframed topic and outline are already publishable in principle—the key refinement now is to make the review (1) operationally precise (clear definitions + coding rules), (2) methodologically auditable (PRISMA‑ScR-style traceability), and (3) critically diagnostic (explicit “failure modes” for RS–ML in data-scarce tropical lakes, plus a minimum-standards framework).

    Below I give you a more precise title, sharpened RQs, a review methodology blueprint you can actually defend, and a framework/checklist that is clearly motivated by inland-water remote sensing realities (optically complex “Case 2” waters, atmospheric/adjacency effects, domain shift, and validation leakage). I also give you a concrete way to align your Python + LLM extraction pipeline with the intellectual standard you’re aiming for.

    1) Title + positioning (make the scope defensible)

    Recommended working title (slightly tightened)

    Remote sensing and machine learning for water‑quality monitoring in data‑scarce inland lakes: a scoping critical review and deployment framework for tropical systems (Oguta Lake, Nigeria, as a context anchor)

    Why this is stronger:

    • “Deployment framework” signals you’ll produce a usable outcome, not only mapping.
    • “Context anchor” avoids the implied promise of a full Oguta case-study model/results (good, because your outline explicitly avoids that).
    • “Tropical systems” is broad enough to generalize, while Oguta keeps the narrative grounded.

    Background positioning language you can safely use (and cite):

    • Inland waters are feasible to monitor with remote sensing because water color relates to water constituents (phytoplankton, sediments, CDOM), but inland waters are often optically complex and algorithm performance is fragile without careful preprocessing + validation. 1

    2) Operational definitions (this is what makes it “rigorous”)

    If you define these once and apply them consistently, your review becomes auditable instead of “LLM-assisted narrative.”

    2.1 “Inland lakes”

    Define as: natural lakes and artificial reservoirs that are not coastal/estuarine waters (exclude bays, nearshore ocean, fjords unless explicitly “lake-like” with inland focus).
    Justification: inland-water remote sensing has distinct challenges and a mature community framing around them. 1

    2.2 “Water-quality parameters”

    Split into two retrieval classes (this is crucial for your critique):

    • Optically active constituents (OACs): typically chlorophyll‑a, suspended matter / turbidity proxies, and CDOM-related signals (i.e., parameters that directly affect reflectance). 2
    • Non-OACs / weakly optical: nutrients (TN/TP), salinity, DO, BOD/COD, etc., which are often estimated indirectly (proxies, correlations, hybrid context models). 3

    This distinction lets you say something more critical than “people predict TN”: you can evaluate whether studies admit they’re using proxies, and whether validation supports that.

    2.3 “Data-scarce”

    There is no single universal cutoff, so make your review robust by using tiers (and optionally sensitivity checks):

    • Tier A (extremely scarce): < 20 in-situ matchups
    • Tier B (scarce): 20–49
    • Tier C (moderate): 50–199
    • Tier D (data-rich): ≥ 200

    This supports RQ3 without pretending the field has a standard definition. Your argument becomes: “Conclusions about deployability should be weighted differently by tier.”

    2.4 “Transferability”

    Define as any evaluation across:

    • Space: other lakes / other regions
    • Time: other seasons/years
    • Sensors: cross-sensor generalization (e.g., Sentinel‑2 ↔ Sentinel‑3 or Landsat)
      This matches what the inland-water community actually struggles with: domain shift + generalization. 4

    3) Refined research questions (same spirit, slightly more “codeable”)

    Your current RQs are strong. I’d only tighten wording so each RQ maps to extracted fields.

    RQ1 (geographic bias). Where are RS–ML inland-lake WQ studies conducted, and what evidence exists of underrepresentation of tropical Africa?
    RQ2 (targets). Which parameters are estimated (OACs vs non‑OAC proxies), and with what frequency?
    RQ3 (data scarcity). What in‑situ matchup volumes are reported (overall and per‑lake), and how often do studies fall into data-scarce tiers?
    RQ4 (validation leakage risk). Which validation designs are used (random, spatial block, temporal holdout, external lake), and how often are designs likely to overestimate performance under spatial autocorrelation? 5
    RQ5 (preprocessing transparency). How consistently are atmospheric correction, adjacency/glint handling, water masking, and matchup windows reported? (What is most often missing?) 6
    RQ6 (transferability evidence). How often is out‑of‑domain testing performed, and what patterns appear in success/failure? 7
    RQ7 (GIS integration). How often are catchment/morphometry/context variables used as predictors (not just discussed), and in what modeling patterns?
    RQ8 (framework). What minimum methodological + reporting standards should be required to claim deployable RS–ML monitoring in data-scarce tropical inland lakes (Oguta as context anchor)?

    4) Methods section blueprint (PRISMA‑ScR + critical appraisal, without overclaiming “systematic review”)

    Even if you call it “scoping critical review,” you should still report like PRISMA‑ScR. That gives you instant credibility.

    4.1 Conduct + reporting standard

    • Use Arksey & O’Malley’s scoping study structure as the conceptual conduct backbone, and PRISMA‑ScR as reporting. 8

    4.2 Minimum PRISMA‑ScR artifacts you should generate

    • Search strings (appendix)
    • Database list + dates searched
    • Screening flow diagram (PRISMA‑style)
    • “Charting” (extraction) codebook
    • Included-studies table (at least: lake/reservoir, country, sensor, parameter, model family, N matchups, validation type, key omissions)

    PRISMA‑ScR exists specifically because scoping reviews often underreport methods; using it is aligned with your “rigorous + defensible” goal. 9

    4.3 Critical appraisal (the part most scoping reviews skip)

    You can keep it lightweight but explicit: a domain-specific rigor checklist (not a full risk-of-bias tool). This is how you justify “critical” without pretending you’re doing a clinical-style bias assessment.

    A practical scoring rubric (0/1/2) you can defend:

    Dimension0 (not reported / weak)1 (partial)2 (strong)
    In-situ matchupsNot reportedReported but unclear per-lake/seasonClearly reported; per‑lake + seasonal coverage
    Validation designRandom split onlySome grouping but weakSpatial/temporal blocking or external lake test 10
    PreprocessingAC/masking unclearNamed but not reproducibleAC method + settings + masking + matchup window given 6
    UncertaintyNoneOne metric onlyMultiple metrics + uncertainty/limits discussed
    ReproducibilityNo code/dataPartialCode + data or full pipeline described
    TransferabilityNoneSame lake across time onlyCross-lake and/or cross-sensor test 7

    You can then report: “% of studies scoring ≥ X on each dimension.”

    5) The “Oguta Lake as context anchor” section (facts you can cite cleanly)

    Keep this short (as you planned), but make it do real work: motivate why tropical + small/medium lake + human pressure + monitoring constraints matters.

    Oguta Lake: a few defensible anchor facts

    • Oguta Lake is a Ramsar site (Nigeria; designated 30 April 2008) with site area reported as 572 ha, and a seasonally varying water surface area reported on the RIS (180–300 ha). 11
    • The Ramsar description highlights it as a freshwater lake receiving drainage from rivers including Njaba, Utu, and Awbuna, and draining into Orashi, and it explicitly notes urban sewage inputs and sedimentation/deforestation pressures. 11
    • The World Lake Database (ILEC) reports maximum depth around 8 m and mean depth around 5.5 m, and also documents strong dependence of local livelihoods on the lake (water supply, fisheries) and sewage inputs. 12
    • A 2018 field study (weekly sampling June–July 2018) reports common physico-chemical and nutrient-related parameters (e.g., DO, BOD/COD, phosphate, nitrate) and argues for continued monitoring. 13

    Why these facts matter for your RS–ML framework (link to failure modes)

    • Small/near-shore geometry → adjacency effects and mixed pixels become nontrivial, especially for medium-resolution satellites; adjacency correction can materially change retrieved reflectance. 14
    • Tropical haze/cloud + variable aerosols → atmospheric correction choices strongly influence water-leaving reflectance, and inland waters are sensitive at visible/NIR wavelengths. 15
    • Non-OAC priorities (nutrients, DO, BOD/COD) → many parameters decision-makers care about are not directly optical; RS–ML studies often rely on proxies, which demands stronger validation claims than “random split R².” 3

    6) What to emphasize in the “Diagnosis” results (so the critique lands)

    These are the highest-yield critique levers in RS–ML inland water papers:

    6.1 Validation leakage: why random splits can be misleading

    Spatial autocorrelation means nearby samples are similar; random train/test splits often leak spatial structure and inflate performance. This is a known issue in spatial prediction, and spatially-aware CV/blocking is widely recommended. 10

    6.2 Preprocessing opacity: why it matters more than most ML papers admit

    For inland waters, performance is often dominated by:

    • atmospheric correction choice/settings, 15
    • adjacency effect (especially small lakes), 14
    • glint/edge masking and cloud masking (often underreported but decisive for matchups). 6

    So your review can credibly argue: “Many papers are not reproducible in principle, because the reflectance they modeled cannot be reconstructed.”

    6.3 Optically complex (“Case 2”) waters: the mechanism behind fragility

    In Case 2 waters, CDOM and sediments can vary independently of chlorophyll; that breaks simple band-ratio assumptions and increases the need for careful modeling + validation. 16

    7) A deployment framework you can include (practical, tropical + data-scarce)

    This is a concrete “framework/checklist” structure that matches your RQ8.

    7.1 Minimum viable data strategy (data-scarce but defensible)

    1. Season-stratified sampling (at least wet vs dry season in tropical contexts).
    2. Matchup protocol declared (time window, spatial aggregation around station, depth of measurement).
    3. Per-lake sample size reporting (not only pooled N).

    7.2 Minimum preprocessing transparency

    • State sensor level (e.g., Sentinel‑2 L1C → aquatic AC) and AC tool/version/settings where possible.
    • If using aquatic-tailored AC (e.g., ACOLITE/DSF), say so and cite. 6
    • Explicitly discuss adjacency risk for small lakes and whether corrected/mitigated. 14

    7.3 Minimum validation standard (to claim deployability)

    Require at least one of:

    • temporal holdout (train earlier, test later),
    • spatial block CV,
    • external lake validation,
    • cross-sensor validation. This directly addresses the known pitfalls of naive CV in spatial data. 10

    7.4 Minimum uncertainty reporting

    At least: RMSE/MAE + bias + robustness across seasons/trophic regimes (not only a single pooled R²).

    7.5 Transferability protocol (simple and repeatable)

    • Define the deployment domain (lake type, optical class, season, sensor).
    • Test one “domain shift axis” (space/time/sensor).
    • Report performance drop and discuss causes (optical regime differences, preprocessing drift, aerosol regime, etc.). 4

    8) Aligning Python + LLM automation with academic defensibility (without turning it into “LLM wrote my review”)

    This is the key meta-point for your Methods:

    8.1 Use LLMs only for assistive extraction, not authority

    In your codebook, enforce:

    • If a field is ambiguous → mark “NR (not reported)” unless text explicitly supports it.
    • Store evidence spans (page/section + short excerpt) for every high-impact extracted item (validation type, N matchups, AC method, transfer test). This is what makes LLM extraction auditable.

    8.2 A robust extraction pattern (hybrid)

    • Deterministic parsing first: DOI, year, sensor names, obvious metrics.
    • LLM second for fields that are semantically messy: validation design, matchup window, uncertainty discussion, reproducibility statements.
    • Targeted manual verification for: (i) any paper that drives a key claim, (ii) all outliers (very high R² with tiny N), (iii) any paper you cite as an exemplar of “good practice” or “bad practice.”

    8.3 Hard QC checks you already discovered (keep them!)

    Your gist shows you’re already doing the right kind of “sanity constraints” (e.g., impossible year–sensor combinations). Keep that philosophy and explicitly justify it as data cleaning for a scoping review registry.

    If you need a citable example for sensor plausibility checks:

    • Landsat 7 (ETM+) launched 15 April 1999. 17
    • Sentinel‑2A launched 23 June 2015. 18
      So any “1970 Landsat 7” record is mechanically wrong, and correcting it is part of ensuring your evidence map is not corrupted.

    9) High-value figures/tables that “sell” the rigor

    These are worth planning early because they dictate what you must extract.

    1. PRISMA‑ScR flow diagram (even if simplified). 9
    2. World map of study locations (counts by region; highlight tropical Africa).
    3. Parameter frequency bar chart (split OAC vs non‑OAC). 2
    4. Sample size distribution (box/violin; plus your scarcity tiers).
    5. Validation rigor matrix (random vs spatial vs temporal vs external). 10
    6. Reporting omissions heatmap (AC reported? matchup window? uncertainty? code?). 6
    7. Framework/checklist (one-page table) + “Oguta fit check” schematic grounded in Ramsar/ILEC constraints. 11

    If you adopt only one structural change

    Make Section 4 (“Diagnosis”) explicitly organized around failure modes:

    • What makes results look good but not deploy?
      Validation leakage + preprocessing opacity + proxy targets + domain shift.

    That gives your review a strong “critical spine,” consistent with the inland-water remote sensing community’s own framing of challenges and best practices. 1

    Message from gpt-5.2-search

    I am working on writing a rigorous, accurate, academically sound and precise scoped critical review on water quality monitoring and characterization using remote sensing techniques, gis, ml using oguta lake as context. I decided to use python scripting, LLM prompts, etc to speed up the literature review manuscript draft process as much as possible.

    The work has quite advanced with changes in decision as the focus and topic are changed. The report is given in this link: https://gist.github.com/Achionye/14f8232e3c597c997be21fd52ee2c71f

    I have decided to reframe the review as " Remote sensing and machine learning for water-quality monitoring in data-scarce inland lakes: a scoping critical review and framework for tropical systems (Oguta Lake as context)"
    The new outline for this new reframed topic, which can be further refined is "You’re right. The coding/automation is your tooling, not the intellectual standard. The standard is: a defensible scoping critical review with transparent reasoning and evidence. Automation is only there to (a) keep you organized, (b) reduce clerical load, and (c) let you quantify patterns—nothing more.

    Below is a revised set of refined research questions and an article structure written as research directions (what you need to argue, show, and synthesize).

    Refined research questions (tight, non–double-barrelled)

    These are phrased so each can be answered by a review using a mix of: (i) your reading of papers, (ii) simple counting/summary where helpful, and (iii) selected deep dives.

    RQ1 (coverage / bias).
    Where are RS–ML inland-lake water-quality studies conducted, and which regions are underrepresented (especially tropical Africa)?

    RQ2 (what is retrieved).
    Which water-quality parameters are estimated using RS–ML in inland lakes, and how frequently is each parameter targeted?

    RQ3 (data scarcity).
    What levels of in‑situ data availability (matchups/sample sizes) are reported, and how common are data-scarce study designs?

    RQ4 (validation rigor).
    What validation practices are used (and reported) in RS–ML inland-lake studies, and how often do they support credible generalization?

    RQ5 (reporting & reproducibility).
    How consistently do studies report key methodological details (e.g., preprocessing/atmospheric correction, uncertainty, code/data availability), and what are the dominant omissions?

    RQ6 (transferability evidence).
    How often do studies test transferability beyond their training context (across lakes, seasons/years, or sensors), and what patterns emerge from those tests?

    RQ7 (ancillary geospatial context).
    How often do RS–ML studies incorporate catchment or geospatial context (DEM/terrain, LULC, morphometry), and what kinds of integration are actually practiced?

    RQ8 (framework contribution).
    What minimum methodological and reporting standards can be proposed for RS–ML monitoring in data-scarce tropical inland lakes (Oguta Lake as context)?

    Note: “Data-scarce” should be defined once (your choice of threshold), but the review remains an argument grounded in the literature—not a spreadsheet exercise.

    Proposed review article headings and subheadings (as research directions)

    Title (working)

    Remote sensing and machine learning for water-quality monitoring in data-scarce inland lakes: a scoping critical review and framework for tropical systems (Oguta Lake as context)

    1. Introduction — set the problem and the “why now”

    1.1 The monitoring problem (inland lakes, tropical constraints)
    What to do:

    • Explain why inland-lake water quality matters (management decisions, risks, monitoring gaps).
    • Establish why “data-scarce tropical inland lakes” are a distinct challenge (logistics, seasonality, fewer long-term programs).

    1.2 Why RS + ML is attractive, and why it can fail
    What to do:

    • Explain the promise (coverage, repeatability) and the practical failure points (optically complex waters; mismatch between field sampling and imagery; weak validation designs; domain shift).

    1.3 What existing reviews typically do—and what they miss
    What to do:

    • Position your contribution: not “another summary,” but a structured critique focused on rigor, bias, transferability, and deployability in data-scarce tropical settings.

    1.4 Oguta Lake as context anchor (not a case study)
    What to do:

    • One short subsection: Oguta motivates why your framework matters.
    • Explicitly state: you are not presenting Oguta model results here.

    1.5 Objectives and research questions (RQ1–RQ8)
    What to do:

    • List RQs and explain the logic: mapping → diagnosis → framework.

    2. Methodology — make your review trustworthy

    2.1 Review type and scope boundaries (scoping + critical)
    What to do:

    • Define what counts as “RS–ML water quality monitoring in inland lakes.”
    • State what you excluded and why (coastal-only, non-water-quality, etc.).
    • Clarify: you will use automation for convenience but decisions are literature-grounded.

    2.2 Search and selection (transparent, not necessarily “perfect”)
    What to do:

    • Describe how you searched, screened, and arrived at the included set.
    • Provide the flow summary and how disagreements/uncertainties were handled.

    2.3 How you extracted evidence (and how you avoided being misled)
    What to do:

    • Explain what kinds of information you pulled from papers (sensors, parameters, models; and crucially: validation, sample size, uncertainty, reporting).
    • State that ambiguous cases were treated conservatively (e.g., “not reported” unless explicitly stated).
    • If you used automated extraction, state that it was followed by targeted verification for high-impact claims.

    2.4 How you synthesized findings
    What to do:

    • Explain the synthesis strategy: descriptive mapping + critical appraisal themes + framework derivation.
    • If you computed counts, frame them as “counts in the included corpus,” not as universal truth.

    3. Results (Mapping) — what the field looks like (RQ1–RQ2)

    3.1 How the literature has grown over time
    What to do:

    • Show time trend and interpret cautiously (e.g., “increasing attention since …”).

    3.2 Where the studies are (RQ1)
    What to do:

    • Summarize geographic distribution.
    • Identify underrepresented regions explicitly (especially tropical Africa).
    • Keep the claim factual: “X studies in region Y,” not “because researchers ignore Africa” unless evidence supports that.

    3.3 What parameters are most studied (RQ2)
    What to do:

    • Identify the “top targets” (Chl‑a, turbidity, TSS/TSM, CDOM, Secchi depth, etc.).
    • Note underexplored parameters and why they matter (e.g., nutrients, DO) but don’t overclaim causality.

    3.4 Common sensors and model families (descriptive context)
    What to do:

    • Summarize platforms and model families used.
    • Avoid interpreting this as “best,” just “most common.”

    4. Results (Diagnosis) — how strong the evidence really is (RQ3–RQ6)

    This is the “publishable” core.

    4.1 Data scarcity in practice (RQ3)
    What to do:

    • Summarize how many papers report in-situ matchup/sample sizes and what those sizes look like.
    • Discuss why small N is risky and what it implies for reliability.

    4.2 Validation rigor (RQ4)
    What to do:

    • Explain what validation types are used and why some inflate performance (random splits in spatially autocorrelated data).
    • Identify how often stronger validation appears (spatial/temporal/external).
    • Your output should read like: “Here’s what the field does; here’s why it’s a problem.”

    4.3 Reporting and reproducibility (RQ5)
    What to do:

    • Assess whether papers clearly report preprocessing/atmospheric correction, matchup windows, features, uncertainty, and code/data availability.
    • Make this practical: “What would someone need to reproduce this? Is it present?”

    4.4 Transferability evidence (RQ6)
    What to do:

    • Identify how often studies test beyond the original lake/season/sensor.
    • Summarize patterns: when transfer succeeds/fails (even qualitatively).

    5. Results (Integration) — what’s actually done with GIS/DEM and context (RQ7)

    5.1 How often ancillary geospatial predictors are used (RQ7)
    What to do:

    • Report the prevalence of DEM/LULC/catchment variables in RS–ML frameworks.
    • Treat “low prevalence” as a finding: it supports your argument that integration is a gap.

    5.2 What “integration” looks like in practice
    What to do:

    • Distinguish superficial vs meaningful integration:
      • used as predictors vs used as descriptive context
      • included in model vs included only in discussion

    6. Discussion — interpret and position your contribution

    6.1 What the mapping results mean for tropical deployment
    What to do:

    • Connect RQ1–RQ2 to the practical issue: models built in temperate, data-rich contexts may not port to tropical, data-scarce lakes.

    6.2 Why current evidence often overstates reliability
    What to do:

    • Explain how weak validation + small N + underreported preprocessing produce fragile “high accuracy” claims.

    6.3 Where your review differs from prior reviews
    What to do:

    • One subsection explicitly comparing: “Prior reviews summarize; we quantify rigor gaps and propose a minimum standard framework.”

    7. Framework / Checklist for data-scarce tropical inland lakes (RQ8)

    This is your “corrective” contribution.

    7.1 Minimum data strategy
    What to do:

    • Recommend minimum matchup design and seasonal stratification.

    7.2 Minimum validation standard
    What to do:

    • Recommend at least one out-of-domain test (spatial/temporal/external).

    7.3 Minimum reporting checklist
    What to do:

    • Atmospheric correction/preprocessing transparency
    • Uncertainty reporting
    • Code/data availability expectations

    7.4 Transferability protocol
    What to do:

    • A simple, repeatable procedure for testing cross-lake/season/sensor robustness.

    7.5 Oguta Lake “fit check” (conceptual)
    What to do:

    • Show how the framework would guide a study plan for a lake like Oguta (constraints-driven), without presenting empirical results.

    8. Limitations

    What to do:

    • Be specific about what you could not determine from papers due to reporting gaps.
    • Note any limitations introduced by your convenience automation (and how you mitigated them with targeted verification).

    9. Conclusion

    What to do:

    • 5–8 takeaways mapped to the RQs: bias, parameter focus, rigor gaps, transferability gaps, integration gap, and the framework.

    If you want one line that captures the “job” of each major section

    • Intro: define the problem + why RS–ML is fragile in data-scarce tropics + why your review is needed.
    • Methods: show how you built a defensible body of evidence and how you judged rigor.
    • Results (Mapping): what exists and where.
    • Results (Diagnosis): how credible it is (data size, validation, reporting, transferability).
    • Results (Integration): whether GIS/DEM context is actually used.
    • Framework: what minimum standards you propose for tropical data-scarce lakes. "

Inputs are processed by third-party AI and responses may be inaccurate.

Your conversations and certain other personal information will be disclosed to the relevant AI providers and may otherwise be disclosed publicly to help support our community and advance AI research.

Do not submit to our Services any personal information or other sensitive information that you would not want to be shared publicly. By continuing to use our Services, you acknowledge and direct us to engage in such sharing.
